text: So this is for issue six, the recommendation is approve as budgeted. Weber? Aye. Jones-Sawyer? Aye. Lackey? No. Melendez, Stone? Aye. That's out 3-1.
Thank you, Mr. Speaker and members. AB 1538 is up for a concurrence. It was amended to add an urgency clause. This is a district bill and has no opposition. I respectfully ask for your aye vote.

And then the other question I want to ask you, so there's been some concerns about pediatric dental work under sedation. The Dental Board has been looking at the issue. There's some recommendations that are out there. I know there's also a bill and so forth. But I am here in Sacramento. I know that in the work with Sacramento and trying to get access to care for children who require sedation, as well as other people's special needs, we've had real problems with access, particularly to facilities which also didn't have anesthesiologists, so hospitals, etc. The general push from the Department has been, okay, county, let's work together with the local and try to get more of this stuff done in offices, right? So that we don't have to, because we're having a real challenge with payments and other things, payment levels, to get we still only have, I think, one hospital that's still willing to do that. We now have the Dental Board essentially going the other way, saying from a quality care's perspective, we need to, although they're not specifying it has to be in certain types of facilities, it is about having more equipment and anesthesia personnel. I'm concerned about, one of the big concerns is access to care, right? So we have the Dental Board pushing one direction and saying that this is the standard that we're pushing toward, and we have a Denti-Cal program that says, well, actually, what we want to try to do is something different from a payment perspective, how we're going to resolve that. So has that something that the Department has looked at? Because fundamentally, it becomes an access issue, right? If the Dental Board says this is the standard of care, and this is what we're going to hold people's licenses to- Dentists are going to say okay, but now I can't get and, so how can we assure that, I mean, the patients get caught in the middle? So has that been something that the Department has been looking at? And if yes, where are you on that? And if no, tell me you're going to get on it right now.

Madam Chair and Members, Lexi Howard on behalf of the California Judge's Association. We appreciate Senator Hueso's effort in this area. We are supportive of efforts to give access to justice through language access. We understand the people who can't participate in the justice process because of language barriers are denied access and were sympathetic.  We want to move this from a tier eight in the interpreter sort of hierarchy, to something that gets more practical results. We recognize it's not perfect, we're trying to move in the right direction to increase access, and with that, we'd respectfully ask for your aye vote.

Absolutely, themre is nothing in the bill to prevent immediate action to protect other children. Obviously, safety is our number one priority, but my concern, I am committing to speaking to them. But my concern is that our children of color are being expelled at a higher rate than every other child. And so, again, the bill does not prevent anybody from taking immediate action to protect all the children. But I absolutely would love to sit down and talk, I think we've been working well with other interested parties, I think this is the first time that they've come up, they didn't come up in our Assembly section but I would love to sit down and talk.

Thank you Mr. Chairman and thank you member Quirk-Silva for bringing this forward. I was a co-author of very, very similar bill last year and I first ask if you would accept my name as co-author as well? I'll be honored to get to do that with you. And also just a couple of points on this, though, is I agree with you that it will be important for us to partner with both UC and CSU for additional funding. I've done that in the two years that I've been up here. But we also, since UC's representatives give a message to the committee, I have a message to UC which is, there are millions of dollars in savings that need to happen too. It's not just increasing funding, it's making sure that you implement, as the State Auditor found out, the working smarter program, even that your travel policy, if you implemented it state-wide, you'd save $9 million, system wide. I'm thinking of the $270 that's someone's budget for food for a month, or books for classes they want to take. And the reason why a tuition freeze is so important is not only because it will maintain, better maintain the affordability, it's already difficult for CSU, and UC, but because I think you need that constraint on you, to really dive down more into the cost savings. We should be partners with UC, as well as CSU, which has made a little but more progress in this area, but still has a way to go. But I think you need that constraint. Again, not only for the affordability and accessibility for students, but there are millions and millions of dollars annually that need to be from where UC needs to get its spending more aligned. And so I thank the author for bringing this forward, a very good idea from last year. I appreciate an opportunity to co-author it. I just caution us that it's not just this is okay as long as we send more many to UC. I think we send more money to UC as long as they make the changes they need to make to their admissions policies, as well as their spending policies so that we can truly be partners instead of just going for the status quo. So that was a little bit of a lecture, you're welcome to comment on it, but thank you.

Mr. Chair, members, Michael Lane with the Nonprofit Housing Association in Northern California. And among our members are some of the oldest, most successful and productive developers of affordable housing in the nation. And these buildings take advantage of the welfare exemptions been in place over the last three decades is a key component of how we actually finance affordable housing. These building do not create a lot of net operating income. They're not able to pay a large tax bill, so it does create a hardship, through no fault of their own, when some families do have higher income and they receive that tax bill. AB 1193 would simply align the state code around this with the Internal Revenue code at the federal level with respect to tax credits. And it doesn't result in any lost revenues. Because at the time the Board of Equalization approves this welfare exemption and a local jurisdiction approves the project, everyone going in knows that those property tax will not be available or paid. And so there's no loss of revenue here. It's just to make sure that the building owner isn't hit with a large property tax bill without sufficient rents to be able to pay that. Again, we urge your aye vote on this bill. Thank you very much.
Thank you very much, Mr. Chair, and proceed when you are ready.

Mister chair and members Sarah Flocks of the California Labor Federation we're also in support. Our trust funds are Taft Hartley funds purchase health benefits for our 2 million members and a majority of them have contracts with PBMs to provide prescription drug services. So, I'm here to answer any questions of the nature of those contracts, and the need for increased transparency on PBMs.

Thank you, Mr. Speaker and members. Members, I've been a public school teacher for over 30 years. And throughout those years, I know many of my colleagues go to work each morning trying to make sure they make the difference that they can in the lives of the children they educate. And over the years, I've not only seen teachers retire, but I've also seen young teachers leave the profession, many of them frustrated by the demands that have been put on them. Some feeling not supported by parents. Some feeling not supported by administrators. But I struggle today as I stand here and look at Mrs. Weber who I have some much respect for and have heard every time she speaks I stop and listen because I know that her heart's in the right place. But as a long time CTA member, I also struggle to go against an organization that I am a part of, for many many years, over 30 years. But I believe this is a bill at the right time that sends the message throughout California that we are here to do what`s best for children. And adding the additional year may seem like a enormous amount of time, but I first hand have seen teachers dismissed at 18 months, not even two years, 18 months because they need to be notified because superintendents and administrators have said we will only give permanent status to teachers who are 10 plus. Now most of us in train a professional not a 10 plus within our first year, and if we really know anything about professions we know we evolve and we continue to learn. Not only within a year, but over decades. So this year I have also heard administrators say to me, this could really make a difference. So, I stand again, which I know there will be some possible teachers and the California Teachers Association who is going to be disappointed, but I believe it's the right bill at the right time, and I am proud to support my colleague, Shirley Weber, for AB 1220.

Good afternoon Mr. Chair and members. Usha Mutschler on behalf of the California State Sheriff Association in respectful opposition to AB 931. We just want you to let you know that the 58 elected sheriffs around the state certainly understand the sentiment that appears to be driving this effort. But however, AB 931 would mandate that every agency's policies be revised. And tens of thousands of peace officers receive extensive, new training. This proposal does not contemplate the cost, time, and resource impacts on agencies. Perhaps, the only thing worse than converting to this standard, which will jeopardize the lives of peace officers and those who they are sworn to protect. Is a mandate that this change be done without any time to adjust policies and properly train peace officers. For these reasons, we respectfully ask that you vote no on this policy, thank you.

Good morning, Madam Chair and members. AB 324 simply closes a loophole, that can make it difficult to prosecute disorderly conduct or acts of invasive voyeurism. This offense consists of photographing or recording an individual in a state of partial or full undress and without their consent. And this removes the requirement that makes that crime more difficult to prosecute. I respectfully ask for an aye vote.

Well, thank you, I'm Mike Dayton. I'm the Deputy Director at the Department of Emergency Management in San Francisco. I really appreciate the opportunity to be here today, and I really appreciate your interest. I look forward to sharing some of San Francisco's approaches to emergency management and some of the things we're doing to improve our overall preparedness to a catastrophic earthquake. Dr. Parrish kind of gave you the grim assessment of earthquakes. It's a reality to us. It's not a matter of if, it's a matter of when. This reality drives our sense of urgency when it comes to preparedness. And to put the risk in context, let me just give you what we anticipate locally, as some of the impacts of just a 6.9 earthquake on the San Andreas Fault. Over 340,000 people would be without potable water. Over 250,000 people would be without electricity. We'd have a major disruption, as you noted earlier, to our transportation infrastructure, which will impede access for mutual aid resources. We'd have over 100,000 displaced households. We'd have close to 60,000 people seeking shelter. We'd have hundreds of fires and collapsed structures throughout the city. So, this afternoon, I would like to highlight a few of our major exercises, some of the significant mitigation investments the city has made in our community preparedness efforts, and conclude with some of the initiatives we're pleased to be working on with Cal OES. So with respect to our exercise program, I'd like to touch on two of the most significant, Fleet Week and Epicenter. Fleet Week is an annual exercise we hold every October. So we do this in partnership with the Department of Defense. This year's full scale exercise will focus on medical surge. We anticipate that in the first week following a major earthquake, hospitals will become inundated with patients with an assortment of injuries and illnesses requiring treatments. Depending on the magnitude, the casualty count could become extreme enough to reach beyond the capacity of the city. Thereby providing no room for trauma patients that continue to arrive with urban search and rescue crews and other response elements. So in this scenario, the Zuckerberg San Francisco General Hospital will make a determination to erect a field care clinic. The field care clinic will alleviate some of the overflow. Patients with critical care needs would still arrive regularly. So in this instance where the city that has no more trauma capabilities the San Francisco General Hospital, in coordination with the city's Department of Public Health. And our Department of Emergency Management; would submit a mutual aid request to the state for state and federal medical support. So these would include the California AMSA medical facilities, the California National Guard security elements and the Department of Defense Shock Trauma Platoons. In addition to this full scale exercise, this year's two day senior leader seminar will focus on the work we have done with the Port of Oakland, Cal OES, FEMA, and the United States Coast Guard, focusing on port resumption. You raised transportation earlier and Ms. Curry mentioned our unique challenges in the Bay Area in terms of transporting over water. It's a particular concern for us. Because on any given day in San Francisco, if the event happens during work time, we've got about 450,000 people that come into the city everyday to work there either commute and visitors. So we would have to get upwards of 400,000 people evacuated out of the city, or either shelter in place. And then an additional challenge is also bringing people into the city that are disaster service workers or part of the mutual aid response system. Part of our exercise this year will also focus on fuel. Obviously fuel is a major dependency in response and recovery efforts. Our second major exercise is epicenter and it is always focused on our earthquakes in our area. We hold it every April just prior to the anniversary of the 1906 Great Quake. We've got a great partnership with the Salvation Army and the American Red Cross. And it's really more of a mass care and sheltering, focusing on the immediate human needs in the aftermath of a disaster. Last year we had the California Earthquake Authority that's here today as one of our sponsors so I appreciate their help. Last year we focused on transportation, infrastructure, mutual aid, mass care, housing and human services and long-term recovery. In 2008, we'll build on this work, and strengthen our relationships and plans to provide mass care and feeding to the displaced populations, clear debris and restore priority transportation routes, better prepare the financial district to care for the workforce, and also engage grocery stores, food distribution centers to maintain a continuity of their supply chains because it's not unique to San Francisco. This is also similar to Los Angeles where you have all the major food distribution centers and warehouses on the other side of faults. And we can either rely on FEMA and Cal OES to provide the calories that San Franciscans need, or people in Los Angeles where we can make our transportation lines more resilient due to the advanced planning to take advantage of grocery inventories that are near by. So we're probably taking the second route or putting an emphasis on the second avenue. With respect to some of the significant investments to San Francisco's infrastructure to make it more resilient, I'd like to note that the city has invested more than $9 billion in seismic retrofits since the Loma Prieta earthquake in 1989. Some of the examples of this include the earthquake safety emergency response bond program. This is focused on retrofitting hospitals, our fire stations and our alternative water suppression system that the fire department relies upon to put out fires after an earthquake. This also includes the water system improvement program. This was a $4.8 billion capital upgrade project which has made the water system more resilient and as Dr. Parrish noted in his presentation. But despite the significant investments and infrastructure, we know that community preparedness would be the key to empowering neighbors to help one another in the immediate stress of a catastrophe. We have several programs aimed at engaging and empowering the community. First we have the SF72 program, it is a people center vision of emergency preparedness. I mean nobody likes to think about emergencies but we tried with SF72 to take the mystery out of what is the process where you can get yourself and your family prepared. We've partnered with Google on this and we created City 72. So basically the design of our program is available for free for other communities to adopt. We've seen cities across the country adopt a similar preparedness program. Secondly, we have the Neighborhood Emergency Response Teams that I think you're probably all familiar with in your communities. And maybe CERT teams or Community Emergency Response Teams in your neighborhood. But this was an outgrowth of the Lorna Prieta earthquake as well. So this is a free program to our residents to learn basic life-saving skills and to participate as a response entity in their neighborhood, as a neighborhood response team. And then finally emergency management is really a team effort. It takes cooperation as Tina Curry said, from all disciplines in the whole community at all levels of government. Having said that, I'd just like to commend Tina and Ryan and all of their colleagues at Cal OES for just a few of the important initiatives they are leading. We talked about earthquake early warning, Anne Kronenberg, my boss, Executive Director of the Department, is thrilled to be on the advisory board. Our biggest things are to get early warning to the schools and to the fire stations. And then also the Bay Area Catastrophic Planning Effort that Cal OES is leading, we're pleased to participate in that. And then the Emergency Management Mutual Aid Program is basically the emergency managers construct to be able to send our emergency managers to help other counties in need. Since 2015, we've sent about 20 city employees to help out fires through out the state. And then just lastly, I know you're crunched for time but I would like to mention just two mitigation policies that the city has adopted. In 2010, we had a community action plan for seismic safety study. It lead to the adoption of mandatory soft story retrofit ordinance. This legislation requires the evaluation and retrofit for multi-unit soft story buildings defined as wood framed structures containing five or more residential units having two or more stories over a soft or weak story. And permitted for a construction prior to January of 1978. This covered over approximately 5,000 buildings and about 115,000 residents. So almost an eighth of our residents have a greater chance of surviving an earthquake as an outgrowth of that ordinance. And then we also have the Building Occupancy Resumption Program, which allows San Francisco building owners to pre-certify a private post earthquake inspection of their buildings. And this is really just geared to getting businesses back to normal more quickly after a disaster. But with that, again I really appreciate your interest and appreciate the partners here so thank you.

text: Thank you, Mr. Speaker and members. AB 434 is back on concurrence from the Senate. This is a bill that is to ensure that all Californians, including those with disabilities can better access our state websites. In 2016, the State Auditor found that we were doing a terrible job of being accessible in our online services to constituents. And this bill is to improve that. The Senate amendments improve the bill, and make it even more efficient and less expensive for the state to comply. I respectfully ask for your concurrence. Thank you.
Thank you Madam Chair.
Thank you, Madam Chair.
Thank you, at the appropriate time, we'll entertain a motion and until then, I thank you very much. Thank you, Chair. Assemblymember, Quirk. I'll say it quickly before anyone can walk in the door.

Thank you, ma'am. Hi, Committee Members. Again, my name is Ron Halog. I'm the Executive Director for CRIL, Community Resources for Independent Living, and also a member of the Lanterman Coalition. We are in support of the Lanterman Coalition. Just to let you know, I heard a two-bedroom for $1,400 in Stockton area. In Hayward, it's one bedroom at $1,600. And so, it not only has to be affordable and accessible, but also subsidized. And so, for people to live in our area, we have to make it work. And again, I believe in sustainability of this and fully support the Lanterman Coalition.
My name is Lynn Lambrecht and the founder of The Living Planner. And I am a board member of NAWBO in San Diego.

Thank you, Madam President. AB 2735 prohibits English learners in middle and high school from being excluded from core curriculum courses. Research over many decades, shows that English learners can experience limits on their opportunity to learn.  Additional research has shown that English learners have been prohibited from taking core courses, and instead are enrolled in remedial courses, which do not provide full access to the Core Curriculum. AB 2735 makes it clear that English learners must have the opportunity to learn by studying the Core Curriculum in our schools. This is a necessary step in ensuring that all of California students have the opportunity to learn. I respectfully ask for your aye vote.

Mr. Chair and members. ... on behalf of the California Medical Association. We are not formally opposed to this bill. We do recognize a lot of the work both on the author's staff and the Committee staff that went into this bill. We do still have some concerns regarding educational opportunities in the rural and kind of solo, small group settings. I think it's easy for a healthcare system like UC or Kaiser to implement certain standards, but a rural doc who doesn't have access to the same type of resources. We just want to make sure that there's no unintended consequences in legitimate above board educational activities, would be prohibited under this bill. And so this concern's been raised with the author, and we continue to look forward to working with them on some sort of resolution. Thank you. Mr Chair, Kristen ... with the California Rheumatology Alliance. We are opposed unless amended. With the amendments we were getting closer. We still have some questions on the amendments. There is some uncertainties, as were raised. So we look forward to working with the author's office and his staff to get those questions answered. Thank you.
I move AB 653. Our final item is file item 5, AB 653 by the Chair. There's been a motion by Mr. Quirk. You may proceed.
My name is Isaac Gala and I strongly oppose this bill as amended.

Move the bill. I second. Well, I have a question. Mr. Chair, I just want to say thank you to the author. As you know, I've been trying to keep myself very involved in trying to, in this space of trying to increase the amount of home stock that we have available, especially for new homeowners. My only concern is I represent a district that was heavily hit by the economic down turn and if not for institutional investors some of our neighborhoods would have been completely eviscerated and filled with squatters. So I'm going to vote against this bill. It isn't because I don't believe in the merits of supporting the idea of creating more stock for new homeowners. But I would be very reluctant to discourage any institutional investing when really they came and just save neighborhoods in my district.

And us trying to create something, at least maybe, a different training for members that really focuses on the type of experiences that we have that's different in putting us in some of those more problematic situations, and kind of working us through some of those situations is a terrific idea. I'm very glad that you brought that to the forefront of kind of how irrelevant the training is that we hear here.

You got it, okay. And then with regard to number five, I'm looking at, you've got a variety of provisions in the bill. Requires citizen counties to do the following. One of them, on my list it's number five, it doesn't determine the contract doesn't displace employees. Does that mean if it does, they wouldn't be able to contract out or they could move that employee over to another position?

Thank you Madam President, Senators. I wish to adjourn in memory of a young man in my district, Stephon Clark. Two weeks ago, Stephon was shot and killed by two police officers in his grandmother's backyard after being pursued by law enforcement.  Stephon Clark was 22 years old and a father of two boys, ages 1 and 3. His family described him as a fun loving, outgoing, and devoted to his family. He loved football, video games, and shoes. And while he experienced many challenges, he was trying to turn his life around. Last week Stephon's family said their final farewell to their beloved grandson, father, brother, and fiance. And we here in Sacramento are wrestling with what his life represents and how he died. Stephon Clark grew up in underprivileged neighborhoods in Sacramento, one of the most diverse cities in one of the most diverse states here in California and our country. Unfortunately, black and brown people often fear law enforcement, but we should not be surprised. Black men are 13 times more likely to be shot than white men. And black men have disproportionately died at the hands of law enforcement. We clearly have a big problem here in America when we've seen it played out in too many deaths of black men and women. And we have a big problem right here in my beloved City of Sacramento. Stephon Clark's death like too many before him, was a preventable tragedy.  Our city leaders and Police Chief have quickly made public the videos of the event and have sought an independent investigation by the Attorney General. And I have to believe my city will have the leadership and strength of will to confront and solve this problem.  And we may need your help to do so. But Stephon Clark cannot be another statistic. His family and community need more than our thoughts and prayers. So let us remember Stephon Clark as a man whose untimely death also led to the end of an injustice. Thank you.

Members just to add on to that, yes the decisions must remain within the confines of state law, but the Commission does retain a white swath of discretion and so this is a very important policy body in our state regarding transportation matters and I certainly ask for your support on this bill.

Thank you. And I'm asking because I really want to know. This is not a gotcha question. According to what I have, these enhancements, a judge has the discretion to get rid of an enhancement for assault with a firearm on a police officer. Is that correct?
Thank you, Madame Chair. Members, Marty Veranacar, on behalf of the California District Attorney's Association. We're in support of this measure.

That bill has four votes, it's up but we will hold the roll open. Thank you very much and thanks for your patience. All right, next up is File Item 11, we're in file order but you're here, and so File Item 11 it is. Do we have anyone else here in? Nope. We will proceed, good afternoon Assemblymember.

Mr Chair and members, Paul Gladfelty on behalf of Caterpillar Inc and its dealers. Our dealers are in the business of selling equipment. However, there are economic downturns, as we all know. They have good customer relations, want to continue those customer relations. And sometimes, there are occasions when they have to take back equipment. These are all friendly take backs, as a practical matter. It's only a handful that they do take back. And as the gentleman from Mr. Deere said, they're actually saving money by doing that. And it's done in a friendly manner. So with that, we have not seen any type of issues over the last four years with the current law. And would suggest that we continue it without the sunset. So thank you very much.

Thank you, please. My name is Emily Rusch. I'm the Executive Director of CALPIRG, the California Public Interest Research Group, statewide consumer group. In recent years, our health insurance rate watch program has reviewed dozens of health insurance companies' rate filings at the Department of Insurance. And we found prescription drug prices to be a significant driver of premium increases overall. I want to start by applauding the members of this Committee and the members of the Senate Health Committee for embarking on a number of strategies to lower the high cost of prescription drugs. Including the vote you held earlier today on SB 17. We believe that this bill, SB 790, is a really important complement to the other efforts that you have been undergoing. Every year in the United States, $73 billion is spent on brand name drugs, for which an equivalent generic is available at a significantly lower cost. $73 billion. Common sense tells us that pharmaceutical companies spend $1.4 billion on gifts to doctors in California, because they know it'll influence their prescribing habits. So if we're going to lower the high cost of prescription drugs and that effect of that on our premiums here in the state, we want to make sure that doctors aren't unduly influenced by brand name prescription drug marketing habits. It was our study back in 2008 that Senator Mcguire mentioned, playing by their own rules, that analyzed the prescription drug companies' voluntary gift limits. And concluded that manufacturers have created limits that, in many cases, fail to constrain their actions at all. We think that this bill is a common sense approach. As Senator Mcguire noted, there's been a number of amendments taken, and we strongly urge your support. Thank you.
So 11- 4. Item number 24, Mr. Gibson, AB 28... We just did that, I'm sorry. Item number 24, AB 20 863, Mr. Nazarian. Please open the roll, call the absent members.
Sure, so I, there. She's super creepy. Yeah. I'm glad she's looking at you, though.
Very good. This is not ours. If you could give me just one moment.

Dr. Barsky, maybe using your metaphor is a good one for my question. There are many who view the failure rate of our students in the academy as a crash that's been going on for decades now. Where tens of thousands of students who have followed all the rules, gotten all the grades, the test scores, been admitted, are crashing out of our universities with nothing to show for it except for debt. And this is not something that has been obscured certainly to the system leadership, the campus leadership, to the faculty of what the problem has been. It's pretty clear, so I appreciate that consultation is a sensitive thing, an important one, and the process for that is also sensitive and important. But I guess what I'm left a little confused about with your testimony is that this is a serious crisis what's going on on our campuses today in California. And it requires, from all of us, leadership and industriousness to do what it takes to try to get the ship back afloat, so our kids aren't sinking and drowning. And that's the part of it that creates some level of frustration for me. The remedial failures have been out there for a long long time. I mean, the folks in Tennessee didn't just see it yesterday. They saw it more than a decade ago, and tried to do something about it. And they seem to be doing something that's working now. But as it is, we haven't stepped up to try to figure out until more recently. And a proposal's been put forward to try to do something about it in a substantive way. And I think it makes everybody uncomfortable, everybody. If you're a high school math teacher, I think you've probably been pretty uncomfortable for a long time. When you've been passing your kids out and then having them fail, and being able to be college ready in math. So I appreciate the challenges that the faculty are facing to try to get up to speed. And I appreciate that the consultative process may have fallen short. But we have to do something to rescue our kids, our students that are dropping out and failing every year. And that's what leaves me somewhat frustrated by your testimony, to be candid about it.

Mr Chair and members, Gregory Kramer on behalf of Planned Parenthood Affiliates of California. We're proud to co-sponsor Senate Bill 743. We would like to also echo the comments of the former witness, as well. And I would just like to specifically say though since 2013, Planned Parenthood has seen a proliferation of Medi-Cal managed care patients enter our doors. Under the implementation of the Affordable Care Act. In 2013 alone, the number of Medi-Cal managed care patients amounted to approximately 8% of our total patient base. But now, at least according to 2016 numbers, 31% of our patients served are under Medi-Cal managed care. So in short, we have seen a fourfold increase in these types of patients. And so Senate Bill 743 ensures that patients across the state may receive the reproductive healthcare that they desire. At their preferred and trusted provider, like Planned Parenthood. So with that, we respectfully ask for your aye vote.
Griselda Barajas, the chair of the Stockton Boulevard Partnership.
Thank you Vice Chair. Ms. Friedman?

Thank you, Mr. Speaker and members. I'm pleased to present to you today, AB 1444. This is a bill that will authorize the Livermore Amador Valley Transit Authority to conduct a pilot project for testing of autonomous shuttles in Dublin, California. And specifically it will connect the bar transit stations to commuter stops and neighborhoods throughout the trivially area, particularly in Dublin. This bill builds on legislation that this body adopted last year that will test autonomous shuttles in neighborhood settings and in office settings. This is the natural area to test so that we are sure to have a connection with that last mile shuttle technology. It has received great bipartisan's support and I respectfully ask for your aye vote.
Well, thank you, thank you so much for being here.
Thank you very much, Mr. Chair. Very pleased to be here to present this bill today.
Chiu? Here. Chiu, here. Maienschein? Here. Maienschein here. Chau? Choi? Gloria? Here. Gloria here. Limon? Wood.

My main concern is obviously safety, and the last thing I want to do is see one of my firemen go down. And when they don't have a landline and their cellphone doesn't work, and the walkie-talkie, or whatever you call it, is not working. And they could help prevent a fire or not prevent somebody from dying. That's why I'm really concerned about, I want to see FirstNet or something work. Because there's nothing worse, and I wouldn't want to be their shoes for a minute. And so I'm very protective of our public safety, as you are, as all of you are. So I appreciate what you're doing. But the FirstNet, I'm kind of stumped that it hasn't moved along farther. It's been four years, five years since I testified. Yeah. And it affects federal agencies. So for instance, outside of Winters, we have a dam. If we don't have connectivity there, it affects Travis Air Force Base. So it seems to me we have a good linkage for some of these places as long as it keys into federal projects. So I'm just curious, as that goes on, I would really be appreciate it if we were informed.

It's a larger question, but let me just give a couple of examples of ways to maybe think about it. On the one hand, one issue is a question of time frame. So clearly, in the short run, say for example, during the emergency regs, where people didn't have a glide path to think about it. There was an issue of having to talk about rates to cover fixed costs for folks who hadn't done it. The rate structures are different all across all 411. I mean, there are more flavors of that than there are at Baskin-Robbins. So there are all kinds of different ways of doing rate structures, there's not just one. And in trying to raise rates and do that with your customers, you do have to spend a lot of time talking and we don't make it easy in California. So it does take a lot of work and it takes a lot of time, and I think there was a lot of pain in the short run during the drought. This is talking long-term. Long-term, it is much cheaper to be able to integrate efficiency at a reasonable level, so you can put off building more facilities, you can put off bringing more water. We're talking about a longer term play to integrate efficiency into our long-term planning. And just speaking from my experience as a public works director, where I had to raise rates and get bonds passed and do all of that, we invested in conservation, because we knew that would extend the life even of our wastewater facilities. So there are agencies that have spent time trying to integrate what they can do in efficiency, not at a hair shirt level, at a reasonable basic level into their thinking, and they've been able to put off facilities, whether it's a $20 million facility, or a $100 million facility, or whatever. If you can put that off for five years, for 10 years, that's a great benefit to your rate payer. because as you're putting your rates together, it's a combination of what's my cost today, but what do I have to invest in for the future. And the drivers that make water more and more expensive are manifold, depends on where you are, every place has a different mix of water sources. I mean, some people take it from a river they can see, that is not the vast majority of Californians. Most get it from hundreds of miles away, and from multiple sources in a very complex miracle actually, that unique and modern history. But it's expensive and it gets more and more expensive. So to the extent, you can put off some of those investments we know we're going to make. It's going to save money in the long run for the rate payer. So some of the issues you raised, they become difficult to explain to rate payers in the short-term, but people have and you can, it's just not easy.

Thank you, Mr. Chairman and members of the committee. Good afternoon, I'm Cynthia Bartas and I'm a registered environmental health specialist in food safety for over 30 years. Food-borne illness still presents a real threat to public health. In fact, at Thanksgiving, 2016 there were 22 illnesses and three fatalities confirmed by the CDC traced back to turkeys prepared in private homes and donated to a Thanksgiving event in Antioch, California. Commercial food service facilities have key elements which private homes simply lack that help control the environment and prevent food borne outbreaks, including tested commercial equipment for quick cooking, rapid refrigeration, electrical safety, and non-toxic surfaces; five different sinks to prevent cross-contamination when washing hands, cleaning produce, thawing meat, sanitizing dishes, and conducting janitorial activities; sufficient space for safe food preparation activities; a designated person in charge to oversee safe food operations, exclude ill employees, and provide other duties to protect consumers' health. Since inspecting select cottage food operations in private homes, we have encountered a lack of food safety knowledge, inadequate temperatures for water and refrigeration, a lack of proper sanitation controls, the inability to ensure household duties are not simultaneous to food preparation activities, and, unlike commercial kitchens, inspections must be arranged in advance. Most food-borne illness is due to the mishandling of potentially hazardous foods, which this bill would allow. The other major source of food-bourne illness is, of course, people themselves, food handlers who are ill or who do not follow food handling protocols. It is vitally important to ensure foods sold for public consumption is safe for the general public and for those highly susceptible populations, such as the elderly, children, and those who are immune-compromised. I ask for your consideration of the scientific depth of knowledge supporting these arguments and the passionate concern for consumers' health. Thank you and good day.

Thank you, Madam President, Senators, I am proud to present SB 551 today. This bill provides minor but meaningful changes that will increase the California Capital Access programs for small businesses, also known as the CalCAP program, it's a program that provides funding to small businesses in California. As we all know, small businesses are the engines of our state's economy, employing 90% of the state's private sector workers, but far too many businesses in California cannot access capital they need to create jobs and grow their businesses. These barriers are even greater for minority, rural, and woman-owned businesses, in 1994, the CalCAP program was created to help small business obtain loans for which they would not otherwise qualify. In 2011, I carried 981, which would provide additional incentives within the CalCAP program to encourage lenders to continue to lend to small businesses. This bill was signed by the Governor, and to date, the authority has enrolled over 14,300 businesses, totalling over $1.5 billion into CalCAP for small business loans. This bill will increase sustainability and transparency of the CalCAP program, it will keep it going, and it will increase eligibility for businesses to obtain loans, so I ask today that you support this. This bill will also improve transparency in the process by clarifying procedures for recapturing state contributions from CalCAP in order to solidify fiscal solvency for CPRFA, and maintain lender trust and participation in CalCAP for the long term. SB 551 has received bipartisan support in policy and fiscal committees, I ask for your aye vote today, thank you.

Thank you.  Hospitals in California are required to meet certain seismic requiremetns in the mid-1990s. The first of these deadlines is to ensure that the building remains standing in a major earthquake. Over 90% of hospitals in California have met this requirement to date. AB 2190 will address delays experienced by the remaining few hospitals that must meet the January first 2020 requirement in order to keep them from closing. This bill establishes bench marks and guidance for these remaining hospitals should they need an extension. The office of state wide health planning and development could grant up to 30 months' additional time, provided that the hospitals agree to timelines and penalties specified by the department, should they fail to meet the timelines set forth in the bill. In addition, the bill requires that OSHPED report to the Legislature the status of the hospital's seismic category, as well as, requiring hospitals to report that they are aware of meeting the 2030 fully operational requirements. With us today are Catherine Scott representing the California Hospital Association and she's here to field any technical questions on the bill.

Well, thank you for having me. Wonderful discussions so far. I think what's also important, technologies are commitment that's been up here on every panel so far. The equity and diversity across institutions and thinking more comprehensively about student's success. My comments today are also kind of driven out of the conversations we have locally as, especially Chief Student Services Officers in the California Community College system. On what are our frustrations from staff and helping students succeed. We're the largest in the world in terms of a system of higher education. And we serve the top 100% of people in California. And I think that that's an important statistic to think about.  That means we are serving students that are inside K to 12 system through our concurrent enrollment. We are working on formerly incarcerated and system affected individuals. We work with folks that are homeless, that are often in very dire circumstances in terms of their social life. So the question that we have in front of us is how do we best move from a system broadly based on access and enrollment towards a success driven model? And one that's focused on addressing equity. And I think as well because we have the systems here how do we have a more global intersegmental approach across student support systems because it's needed in terms of building a more structured and intentional pipelines for students. There is frustration on how do we move our students from our system as a California Community Colleges into the UCs and to the CSUs and especially in terms of capacity. We need to think about building capacity on professional development on local campuses, not just to know why we need to change but how to affect change at scale especially in areas of integrated student support.  So I have four main points I'm going to focus on today. The first one is about our own transformational demands ahead of us of a California Community College System, which means that we need infrastructure, tools, support, and training for organizational change. In this era of guided pathways which I think is moving us in the right direction in terms of alignment.  And transformational work on community college campuses. As we rethink about how students move through our colleges, there's great need to retrain and restructure organisations. And in particular the challenges involved retraining and reclassifying many classified professionals and student support staff in order to align and restructure institutions and models that best support degree and certificate attainment, as well as transfer and career entry. So we need systemic approaches to helping colleges retrain staff, including professional development programs in the pursuit of student success and equity. For example, we're all ready a Hispanic or Latinx supporting system.  About 40%, I think it's 42% at the particular moment. How do we actually have professional developments to help colleges learn not to be Hispanic enrolled institutions but Hispanic serving institutions? That kind of professional development is really needed across our staff and student support, and how can they best recognize barriers for students and modify our practices locally? Second of all, we need statewide technology including cross-system technology and tools are needed to support integrated planning, program navigation for students, data sharing and tracking. Examples can include electronic catalogs that integrate with curriculum, data dashboards that can track students and their educational plan progress from community colleges to universities. Refine websites for efficient educational planning and career goal setting, infrastructure for prior learning assessment. Programs for online counselling and academic advising and technology for just in time support that is integrated with instruction efforts. So leading transformational chains requires specific skills and communication can be effective for you to sub data and implementation beyond the discussions of why the framework work matters. And California community colleges in and their student support staff broadly meets shared models that can be adapted to individual institutions.  The second broad point that I'd like to talk about, are having clear structural pathways from community colleges to California State Universities and the University of California. With an emphasis on building capacity for local guaranteed admission and a pipeline of degree attainment. And this is a frustration for many of us.  We have those of you that have seen the data from community colleges that move into the University system, many of our graduates are doing much better than those that start at the CSUs or the UCs once they transfer. And so given of the large population of the students in the social net that we provide, how can we have more capacities of very important point for our staff that are trying to move students through and have real effects in their lives. So while the associate degrees for transfer have provided a pathway and guided pathways has really helped us in this effort, many problems remain in terms of alignment, articulation, and providing a true, efficient pipeline For students who could benefit from attending community college with a guaranteed entry into a local CSU or UC. I will say this, that I have a 20 percentage point gap at my institution in terms of underrepresented students that have traditionally not been able to transfer due to not having a local guarantee at the institution down the street.  And I will say that if we are able to market to students that are in the K to 12, when we are doing outreach and student services. And say if you get these degrees at your local community college, you are guaranteed entry into a CSU or UC that is local, that will change for families and it will change for students. So intensive amounts of labor are provided by student support staff and councilors in terms of curriculum mapping across systems and educational planning. Yet many students still end up with a less than seamless experience when transferring institutions, due to a lack of intersegmental agreement. So recommendations include intensive branding of a statewide pipeline of success from community colleges to the CSU and the UC.  A re-conceptualization of the articulation process, including broad intersegmental professional development. And gendering or infrastructure that promotes more community college lower division enrollment, and more capacity in the CSU and UCs for upper division courses. Technological infrastructure for state wide cross system educational planning.  And guaranteed entry into local universities. Third point I would like to make is about redesigning the support for effective advising at scale. We're talking about institutions. In a medium-sized community college, we serve 24,000 students on an annual basis in terms of our head count. There are many that are much higher. And there's a lot of capacity that's needed, in terms of advising students. There's a great to provide effective advising at scale. Currently, counselors provide educational planning at California community colleges including, academic, personal, and career counseling. And now counselors are being asked to complete more transfers in career services, given the vision for success that we have from our Chancellor's office,  which I think is a great initiative. But many colleges have counselors to student ratios of 1 to over 1,000, and the system is not currently designed to perform counseling services at scale. Funding and hiring counselors is extremely difficult on campuses with the structure of the 50% law. So either more funding for counselors is needed or structural changes may need to occur.  That could enable the California community colleges to collaboratively rethink forms of academic advising in order to meet the needs of all students. There are academic advising models using systems outside of California, even state systems like UC or CSU. Additionally, student short centered educational planning for students moving through systems, as well as appropriate intersegmental technology, may warrant investigation and investment. The last point is this. We need more increased funding to holistically meet student needs. Given the students that we serve across all of our institutions, and when you look at a map of where they are serving students, you can tell that we have incredible diversity and need for local populations. The open access mission of the California community colleges is complex and requires the connectedness of programs and services. And an agenda of serving our most in need students, holistically. In order to achieve successful completion of students educational goals, the system needs clear financial and structural support for all students, including mental health services, basic needs including addressing food insecurities, transportation issues, and housing insecurities, disability services. And increased services for disproportionately impacted populations. To effectively serve high need students and increase completion rates,  perhaps the student needs a funding model that supports additional classified personnel. And also faculty advising duties in addition to teaching loads. We need funding for instructional support services, such as tutoring and supplemental instruction, which is very difficult for us to get inside of the current budget structures that we have.  The difficulty remains to effectively assess, address, and intentionally intervene. And followup with students as they enter and move through the college system in holistic ways. So funding community colleges per student in ways that address parity across the systems. And meet the needs of our students in terms of instruction and support is imperative.

Good afternoon, my name is Jenna Gary, and I'm an attorney with Legal Aid at Work. We're a nonprofit, public interest legal organization that advocate on behalf of low wage workers. So I'm honored to be here today as a co-sponsor and express strong support for SB 937, and I want to thank Senator Wiener for his leadership on this important issue. Simply put, SB 937 will ensure all lactating parents are able to meet the most basic needs of their child, providing food. At Legal Aid at Work we have a work and family help line, where we hear from about 1,500 to 2,000 employees across the State of California, who are struggling with being parents and employees. And far too often we hear from individuals who lack proper, adequate lactation supports. For example, I just want to share one story of our client Tara. Tara worked as a waitress at a restaurant when she had her first child. After returning to work Tara wanted to continue breastfeeding her child and asked her employer for lactation accommodations. Unfortunately, Tara was told the only space they had was a refrigerated wine room with no access to electricity, no place to sit, no place to put the pump. And so she was left with the sole option of either stopping breastfeeding or quitting her job and risking severe financial hardship. An employer should not be able to take that decision away from a new parent. SB 930 will help ensure parents like Tara are able to make the right decisions for themselves and their children. SB 937 provides a simple solution to address these critical disparities in health and equity that were discussed here today by outlining minimum standards for the space and practices that will ensure that workers receive supportive lactation accommodations. Studies show that 6 months employed parents receive lactation accommodations are 2.3 times as likely to exclusively breastfeed compared to those who do not have adequate lactation supports at work. Similarly, at 6 months, employed parents receive breastfeeding accommodations are 1.5 times as likely to exclusively breastfeed with each passing month compared to those who don't. Thus for these reasons, we respectfully ask for your aye vote on this important legislation to support breastfeeding parents and their children.

So thank you for having me, good morning. Jennifer Kent Department of Healthcare Services. I think just starting more broadly, when I took over as director we had done a stakeholder survey that was posted publicly on our website. And I read it as I came into the department because I wanted to see what various opinions were. And I will stipulate to what Alice said earlier, is we're a big department with a lot of complex programs. We can be really difficult to work with from the standpoint that we use acronyms, like it's own language, that most people, we forget that other people don't speak the language of department bureaucrat. And so I took that feedback, and I listened to a lot of the comments where people said we give feedback to the department and we never hear back. Or we don't understand why the decision was made that was counter to what our suggestion was. And so one of the things that I took away from that, which is if you're going to go to the effort of giving the department feedback, it is our intent always, as much as we can to the best of our ability to try to explain the comments that we received, as well as the reason for why we either changed our policy based on those comments or why we couldn't. We recently had a document that we shared with hundreds of stakeholders, not on children with special healthcare needs in particular, but we asked for feedback within 30 days. And in that case we had completely conflicting opinion on the exact same topic, right. So one group said, please do it this way, and the other group said, no, we want you to do the complete opposite. And so sometimes when you're searching and asking for recommendations, we ultimately have to go back and make a decision. But I think we try really hard and I'm committed to doing this which is explaining why we couldn't or we could do something. As it pertains to both the CCS Advisory Committee, as well as MCHAP, I'll speak to MCHAP first. When I started, that was kind of the first year after the advisory panel had come into the department. They were very familiar with Healthy Families. The board, the Medical Insurance Board was a very different entity than the department. And when we talk about training and education, it seemed important to me that the Committee, no matter who they were, family members or providers or county representatives or hospital representatives, because the Committee has a whole set of those different organizational memberships, that sometimes they were trying to make recommendations to the director, to me, but they didn't actually understand maybe the background or some of the finance scene behind the programmatic area that they were interested in. So we've done, and this is I think helping as we do agenda setting, multiple agendas in a future way. And then what we do as a department is we provide deep dive kind of subject matter experts where we come into MCHAP, we have our experts on either mental health, or dental, or managed care, whatever. And they provide both training and kind of ground setting for the entire Committee so that when they come back to me with a recommendation, they are informed about the program and kind of how it operates so that they can then make certain recommendations. And that has gone, I think, really well. It has made their recommendations incredibly meaningful because they are looking at how we operate today and then they're coming back with concrete solutions rather than just saying, fix it, that's not helpful. And so I think that has been particularly evident in the quality of the recommendations. And then consistent with my belief is that when they give me a written recommendation, I sit back with them the next meeting and say, here's what we are prepared to do, here's what we are doing, here's why we can't do a certain thing. Or this is our alternative for you to consider. And so I've been pretty pleased with how that has been going. We recently, with Alice, resigning from MCHAP. We just concluded interviews last week for both a pediatrician representative as well as a family member representative. So our next MCHAP Advisory Committee when you attend will have new members. So swearing in big happenings. For CCS in particular, again a very important program, oldest in our department by a long shot since 1927. The Advisory Group was formed specifically around changes that were being contemplated to CCS in 2012, 2013. I was actually on the Advisory Group before I became the director, and as CCS has evolved from that initial formation it is now kind of permanently in place within our department. There's kind of a two-track process that we're working on, both the whole child model, which are the county organized health system models that we're working on to better integrate the CCS services with the rest. But there's also another track within the CCS Advisory Group around performance metrics and quality outcomes. And that is composed of both the provider organizations, neonatologists, hospitals, family members, researchers. And the idea is is that we are trying to look at CCS more broadly but also more specifically on both the kind of historical CCS program as supposed to the whole child model. When we negotiated 586 last year in the Legislature with a lot of people in this room, as well as Senator Hernandez, one of the things that we had felt strongly about from the statewide perspective was having advisory groups at a state level informing us as a department. So we worked with Dr. Hernandez, as well as the stakeholders, to have each county organized health system have a advisory group on a local level composed of CCS parents and providers. And so I think we recognize, and this was certainly from the previous panels, statements around all politics are local, all healthcare is personal. So people's very, very personal experiences with healthcare help inform Healthcare systems no matter whether they're at a state level or at a local level. And so we have been both supportive of that as well as working with the plans to make sure that they embrace what the intent of that was in the legislation. So I will just kind of say broadly, and I'm happy to answer questions on the challenges around family engagement. Obviously, the ability for us to have families, sacrifice time out of their home if they're being a caregiver full-time for their children, or to travel the Sacramento is certainly difficult. And this has been something that we're trying to work on is, if we just put a family member into one of our advisory groups and immediately start talking, it is really, I can imagine, incredibly overwhelming and so I've actually had conversations with the chair of MCHAP Committee which is what this new family member that is coming in. We need to be very intentional and very thoughtful about working with her to make sure that she's brought up to speed, that we give her as much material ahead of time. But also making sure that we keep an eye on the conversation as the Advisory Committee is even meeting to make sure that she's not just kind of completely lost. And so I think being aware of who's on your Committee at all times and making sure that you try to not necessarily lower the conversational level but just make sure that your information and that your time is afforded to every single participant in a way that is meaningful. So anyway, those are some of the good things. And obviously, again, being a big department, we struggle to get it right. But I would like to think that we are evolving our feedback loop as much as we can, so thank you.

Thank you. Thank you Mr. Chair, Ryan Woolsey with the LAO. Your agenda was two questions for us to address so I'll just provide some brief responses to those. First, a brief history of the Fingerprint Imaging System in CalWORKS. Budget legislation related to the 1995-96 budget required the administration to design, implement and maintain a statewide fingerprint imaging system to be used in a predecessor program of CalWORKS to defend dependent families with children, and in the food stamp program now known as CalFresh. And the system was subsequently rolled out in all counties in March of 2000. In 2003 the Bureau State Audits released a report with several conclusions. The first of which is that the state fingerprint imaging system was implemented without determining the extent of duplicate aid in the programs. Which made it unclear whether the cost of developing and maintaining the system was justified by the savings that were achieved. And I'll note that currently, the state fingerprint imaging system detects a relatively small amount of duplicate aid today, the savings of which related to which is far less than the ongoing operating cost of the system. Where there's not as much clarity is, how much duplicate aid is prevented which potentially could result if the system were removed. This audit was followed up by subsequent audits in 2009 and 2015 in which the Auditor suggested that the Legislature Consider whether to continue the fingerprint imaging system by looking at whether the system is justified by the avoided duplicate aid. But also by considering other issues related to potential stigma for Applicants that are asked to go through the fingerprint imaging process. Finally, in 2011 the Legislature enacted language that ended the use of the fingerprint imaging system for the CalFresh program. So today it is usediIn the CalWORKS program and it also is used by counties and in some cases their general assistance programs. The second question asks what is the current system of verifying the identity of CalWORKS applicants. CalWORKS applicants are generally required to provide a Social Security number when they apply or proof that they are applying for a Social Security number within 30 days of application. Applicants must also provide proof of their identity using several allowable documents, which include but are not limited to things like a driver's license or state ID card, or another for of photo ID or example from a school. Passports are also allowed, immigration documents can be used as well. This information is collected and then is verified against certain federal data sources through what's known as the Eaves system. Key data systems that are used to verify identity information include the Social Security Adminstration which looks at the name, date of birth, and Social Security number. And then also, against Immigration Services databases for, checking for lawful status. That's the information I have in respect to the questions. I'm happy to try to answer any other questions you may have.

Good morning Madam Chair and Committee Members, I'm Judy Taschner form the San Diego District Attorney's office. As prosecutors in the sex crimes and human trafficking unit, we see first hand the devastation and destruction brought on by life in the world of pimping, pandering and human trafficking. Vulnerable victims are sold the dream of love and a better life by their pimps. Yet they experience the opposite. The dynamics of this are very similar to domestic violence. And victims end up trapped with few other options. The viability of getting out of this life and have a brighter future, it can depend on the ability to break free of the power, control, and manipulation of the pimp. AB 1735 is a simple tool that can be given to our criminal justice system to help these victims, and to give them the space, the freedom, and security in order to break out of the life. And who are these other victims, who are these victims that are trafficked for non-sexual purposes? This bill applies to labor trafficking victims. These victims are mostly immigrants working in the U.S on temporary visas. They work in agriculture, hospitality, construction and the restaurant business. They've experienced some type of force, fraud, coercion, which can include document fraud, withholding of their documents, extortion, abuse, discrimination, psychological manipulation, violence and threats, against themselves and their family members. There are high rates of civil labor exploitation, where the employer and the trafficker controls their housing, their food, their transportation, and their immigration status.  Many of these foreign workers are lured by false promises of good jobs and benefits, and these traffickers often can be related to friends, family, and their community members. These are the vulnerable victims that we're seeking to simply have included in this optional restraining order, post-conviction, to give judges the option of protecting them for up to 10 years, which is available to other victims, as well. Thank you.
