Court can release on bail. Can raise the bail, like they can do now. The court can keep you in jail. The idea is what we are trying to get to here this, Senator Mitchell I think made the point clear that I am, but you've got poor people sitting in jail. One of the people that testified said that the current bail system is effective in keeping poor people in jail. What we're really trying to get to is the injustice. What was mentioned, with respect to the opposition, at the time of 12% of the people in Los Angeles, who they estimate can't afford bail. Okay, what if that's your brother or your sister or your kid, or your aunt or your uncle? Justice is not about the size of your wallet. So we're not taking the judicial branch and the power of government. What we're trying to do is to focus in that narrow area. And I personally think that there is opportunities to work with the bail people. because these people who are in jail don't have the money. They're not customers, right, so we're trying to deal with that. There's not any effort, I'll certainly let the other witnesses and my co-author address that. But that's what we're really trying to get to. And so the reason we kept your measures in with respect to 646 and 12 70 from 1999, is because we thought you were right. And you were trying to set a higher standard to make sure that you had to set eyes on these things. That people weren't just released because these violations, you didn't have this repeat situations. And we thought that it was a good thing. That's why we did it and keep it in. So, there is a lot of work that went in this thing. And thinking through the nuances of how to put these together. As you saw the number of cosponsors, the number of people involved in this discussion. It is a complicate issue. But we are informed by Kentucky. We are informed by the District of Columbia. We are informed by the federal government in terms of being in much greater distance ahead of where we were before. The reference was made about Ronald Reagan and Newt Gingrich sitting down. Well back then, there was no Google, there was none of this technology. Nobody had smart phones, people didn't get texts. There were all sorts of things that are available now. What we're trying to do, is to take in consideration the new data, the new evidence, the new tools. And to modernized the system to make sure that justice is not based upon the size of your wallet. And yet still to be sensitive to the issue to issue respect. I don't know if you were here in my opening, but I said, the bill says that public safety and the safety of victims. The people from Crime Victims United were here talking about our priorities here. We're not trying to do anything to make society is less safe. We think we're making it safer. We think we're making more rooms in jails instead of people who are being convicted and sentenced to jail. And immediately being released as there's no jail space cause it's being held by some fella who doesn't have $680 to pay the bail. That's what we're trying to get to.

This hearing of the Senate Committee on Public Employment and Retirement. And I want to welcome everyone here. We have three items on the agenda. And so we will go ahead start a Subcommittee until we have a quorum. But before we begin, I do want to just make mention of our sympathies for the passing of CHP Officer Kirk Griess, who we also just adjourned in memory in our Senate chambers.  Last Friday morning, Officer Griess was killed while performing a normal traffic stop on an SUV on Interstate 80 near Fairfield when another vehicle veered into his location and collided with Officer Griess in the first vehicle. Both Officer Griess and the SUV driver were transported to the hospital, but tragically pronounced deceased. Officer Griess was 46 years old, was a US Marine veteran, and 19-year veteran of the California Highway Patrol. And he had worked at the Fairfield CHP Office for 16 of those years, and was a Vacaville resident. He is survived by his wife and three children. This tragic event once again underscores our first responders. Our first responders put themselves in harm's way every day to keep us safe. And that's why we do the work we do in this Committee, to safeguard the benefits for public safety officers and their survivors, and why this is so important. And so on behalf of the Public Employment Retirement Committee of the Senate, our hearts go out to the Griess family and the entire CHP community.  So with that, I would invite Senator Moorlach to come and present. I presume you want to present them in numerical order? So begin with Item Number 1, is SCA 8. Thank you, Senator Moorlach.
Aaron Binecher, I'm from Sacramento, and I oppose this bill.
Certainly, but then allow me to- Well, I still- I'm sorry, apologies
Good morning, Mr. Chair and members, Christina Dicaro representing the California Chronic Care Coalition.

Good afternoon, thank you for having me today. I'd like to begin by thanking this Committee, and also the Legislature. You have been instrumental in providing millions of dollars to Fresno County. Right now the county is spending, and we have many different projects. Removing dead trees along roadways, along public areas within Fresno County. Just to make sure that we have adequate ingress and egress, if we do have a catastrophic event, a fire up in eastern Fresno County. Regularly, I'm receiving requests from individuals who own private property, they're low income or maybe middle income, they live on properties. And we just don't have any resources to allocate to individuals who live on their own property up in the rural parts of Fresno County. This bill would provide some flexibility and an opportunity for low income and moderate income individuals to access funds to cut down trees. In some cases, depending on terrain and the size of the tree that has died, it could cost upwards of $2,000 per tree. Many individuals have, it's common to have 60 trees that are dead around their home that need to be removed. And so you can just see the size and scope of cost to remove these trees. And a lot of families just don't have those kinds of resources. I'm here to answer any questions you may have and would appreciate and aye vote on this bill.

I can go first. Good afternoon, my name is Jesse Graywall and I'm a policy researcher and legislative aid for United Food and Commercial Workers Western States Council. So UFCW has played an integral role in the development of medicinal and recreational cannabis legislation and policies, and was the first union to come out and support Proposition 64. UFCW has had the privilege of representing cannabis workers since 2010,  and continues to organize workers from seed to sale in the cannabis industry. From the start of our organizing campaign, Cannabis Workers Rising, we have felt strongly about ensuring equity and inclusion in the cannabis industry. Supporting social equity at the state level looks quite different than many of the conversations occurring at the city level. However, the state does have significant resources at its disposal to support entrepreneurs, workers, and communities affected by the war on drugs, which is the intent and focus of social equity. Community based organizations should be prioritized in all aspects of social equity work given their reach and affected areas. UFCW looks a social equity efforts and four overarching buckets. Supporting business ownership, supporting workers, supporting broader community and reinvestment, and halting future criminalization and prioritizing restorative justice. We laud the work that Senator Bradford and his office are doing through SB 1294 to support social equity work in these four buckets.  To be respectful of time, I will focus on two buckets. The first is supporting business ownership in affected communities through providing access to reduced interest and interest-free loans and grants. Providing reduced or waived licensing fees. Providing technical assistance to state and local equity applicants and assistance in securing business locations. And allowing for sharing of common spaces by licensees. To elaborate more on the importance of this point, we are noticing at the ground level that retail space and area zone for cannabis businesses are skyrocketing in price, and is becoming a significant barrier for who would be equity applicants to start a cannabis business. The second bucket I would like to focus on is supporting workers in affected communities. Through workforce development programs that link affected communities to training, apprenticeship and placement in the industry, funding the division of labor standards and enforcement in Calosa to protect workers, resource community colleges to create training and apprenticeship curricula specific to the cannabis industry. And incentivizing hiring from affected communities and transitional workers, including post relief services, such as job placement, and wraparound services to ensure the workers' success. The other two buckets that I won't go into are broader community reinvestment and affected communities, and halting future criminalization and prioritizing restorative justice. It's through the lens of these four buckets that UFCW believes that the state must engage the cannabis industry sooner rather than later, to ensure that California's cannabis industry is equitable and inclusive. Especially so those who are ravaged by the war on drugs are able to partake in the cannabis industry's success. Thank you.

Thank you, Mr. Chair. I want to join our colleague, the Chair of our sub-committee number three on resources and transportation in expressing our concerns about the lack of investment in housing. And let me also start by thanking this Budget Committee, and our Chair, and the work of all of us here on this. As our colleague from Santa Monica just alluded to, the fact of the matter is we had prioritized $400 million as a new and additional investment in housing. That was something that was prioritized by this Budget Committee, by our Speaker, by the Assembly, that was not included in the conference Committee process in the midst of the worst housing crisis in the history of our state in recent times. Now, I think we can all talk about a number of reasons why we're in this crisis. And let it be known that we have plenty of bills moving forward to focus on how we streamline housing creation, how we hold jurisdictions more accountable to building housing, how we tackle different aspects of the housing crisis. But one major reason why we are where we are is that at the beginning of the great recession, we were forced to cut close to $2 billion a year of money that we used to invest in housing with the demise of redevelopment agencies and the drying up of housing bond monies. And that is a reason why at this moment we have the highest poverty rate in the country, when you include housing cost. Why we have 1.7 million California families that lack access to affordable housing. And the idea that we weren't able to find $400 million to start making a down payment on addressing this crisis was extremely disappointing. And I think for all of us who serve in different subcommittees, we know that lack of housing is exacerbating not just the impact of, but the cost of how our state addresses poverty, how our state has to address transportation, how our state is not addressing how we build a 21st century economy. How we ensure the protection of the environment, how we ensure the protection of the health and safety of our residents. By not making a small investment in housing, we are exaserbating many, many more costs in all these other areas. So we will certainly be continuing this conversation. But I have to say, this aspect of the budget is one that's been extremely disappointing, and I also want to be on the record on that.

Thank you Madam Speaker. As Chair of the Legislative Black Caucus said, it is a privilege we just had an opportunity to hear a great deal of the background on Frederick Roberts but it's our pleasure that we are able to recognize members of his family here today. The grandchildren of his own, his brother William Roberts Descendants of his family and with the help of the minority floor leader and the Freedmen Bureau we were able to make connections to the family and they are here with us today. And as part of the Robert's lineage they too are descendants of Sally Hemings and President Thomas Jefferson, the Author of the declaration of Independence. Robert's descendants are here on the floor with us and we'd like to recognize them now. Allen Wales Haddinett great niece of Frederick Roberts, Robin Johanna Roberts great niece of Frederick Roberts, and Karen Willard great, great niece of Frederick Roberts. Please join me in welcoming the descendents of Frederick Roberts.

text: Madam Chair and Members, Quick on the draw. Louie Brown on behalf of the California Association of Nurseries and Garden Centers, the California Strawberry Commission, and a few others listed in your analysis in support of the bill. And thanks to the author.
Yeah, so for example, this is a perfect example, your comments are well taken. I don't disagree with a single thing you just said. You're absolutely right.
Chair and members, Jonathan Feldman, California Police Chiefs Association, in support.

Thank you, good morning. I want to thank the chair and the Committee for taking up the time on this important bill. Just by way of background, I've been a deputy DA for over 10 years. I've been assigned to the human trafficking unit and helped start the unit when it began about five years ago, and we've now grown to five felony prosecutors. We've handled literally hundreds of cases related to human trafficking, pimping and pandering. And I only put forth my background so that you understand where I'm coming from with this statement in support. The objective behind 1204 is not to change or make substantive changes to the law. It's to utilize existing language in the six theories and also the case law that goes back over a hundred years on the pandering statute by using the language of the case law and the statute. We're making clear that, that the law that applies today will continue to apply with this new simpler version of 266i. The current statute is a good statute, but the problem is it's unwieldy to work with because it's got six different theories. We have to either allege a specific theory of liability or at the time of trial, we have to elect one of the six theories or multiple theories of liability, which makes it confusing for juries, confusing for courts, confusing for prosecutors, and confusing for the defense. And so by placing everything in to one simple to understand statute, it makes it easier to charge, and easier for everybody to be on notice of and to understand. This again is designed to fix the unwieldy nature of it but not just to plant the current law.  We've utilized language from cases like people of Zambia, from the Supreme Court of the State of California in 2011. Cases like Montgomery from back in, I believe 1946 or 1947, and others. Some of the oppositions suggest that this statute if passed would criminalized a young person or a person who's a victim of human trafficking from simply persuading somebody else into the life.  Current law requires and the statute that's a been proposed, requires that anybody who is engaging in the conduct. The active persuasion or the active encouragement has to maintain a specific intent to make that other person be or become a prostitute, this statute doesn't change that. So giving condoms out doesn't actually subject somebody to liability for pandering,  because they would still have to harbor some intent and do something to actively aid or encourage or facilitate it. This does not criminalize an adult sex worker in the matter that I just described. I want to point out that I believe this Committee and this Legislature and the Governor has been very supportive of anti human trafficking measures, and, and I want to thank the Committee for that and the Legislature for that  and the way that they have done so is by making funds available for victim services for pushing the development of vertical prosecution units. By engaging in public awareness campaigns, by providing adequate resources and training for law enforcement, and creating expanded mandating reporter laws and educating educators about human trafficking.  But the Legislature should not give support to the idea that prostituting is a viable way to get out of being prostituted, and I think that was the tenor of some of the opposition to this bill, is assisting somebody to perform acts of prostitution safely would somehow criminalize their activity in a felonious way, and this bill does not do that. I'd also note that there may be some concern that this could be applied to sex purchasers. That if somebody goes out for himself to purchase sex from an individual that they could be liable for pandering, again that's not the current State of the law and that's not the intent behind the statute. There are cases are go back to 1874 in people V Rodriguez that talk about pandering being a felonious conduct only, if you're doing it for a third party. And by using the existing language of the statute and the case law, I think this bill makes it clear that we're not trying to change that, that 1204 doesn't seek to change that, and so the law would be adequate and would not be applied to make a misdemeanor conduct of purchasing sex automatically become felonious conduct. Again, I want to thank the Committee for its time and consideration of this bill, and I'd be happy to take any questions.

Yes. Thank you for bringing this bill. My hairdresser of 15 years is a shop owner. Every time I go to have my hair cut, I am anxious that I'm going to end up looking like our Chair. Maybe someone argue that would be a good thing, I'm not saying it wouldn't. But I do know that, and I was on this Committee when we pass 1513. I don't think it was ever intended to apply to beauty salons. It was intended to apply to farm workers and I think truckers. Those were to two, those were the groups that came in, articulated the reason for the bill. Made sense, I voted for it. Suddenly, my hair dresser's telling me that he is basically this model that he and many of the other hair dressers in my community have been using for decades is going to require that they have to reimburse their workers, even though all of them have been very happy. They've got a good arrangement. He also trains people, so before they become stylists, he's anxious to try to help young people step up. But if he has to pay someone $30 an hour who is a trainee, he said, I'm not going to do it anymore. So this is example. And there are many other things, and I guess the bottom line for me is, this model has worked I think successfully and effectively for people within that business. Both the employer and employee for many years and this I think inadvertence has just really throwing a curve ball to this whole industry. So I am very happy for many reasons, including I'm supposed to get a haircut tomorrow. So if we get home in time, they might even make me look, or get rid of the grey here. I don't know. But I do think that this was one situation where the resolve was absolutely not intended by anybody. And I would ask the Chair to also consider, I think the concern about the $30 an hour for downtime. Takes away that ability of the stylist and the owner to negotiate what the percentage will be for their employees. And so, with that I will support this bill with the understanding that you'll continue in those discussions.

Thank you. Thank you, it's good to see you. This may get a little community oriented but I haven't served at the local level. What about a new license? If a new license is denied, that's a new business. What's the timeline? Do you know? And I can also call the-

Chair and members, Jedd Hampton, representing Leading Edge California. We represent over 400 not for profit providers, the senior affordable housing, assisted living and skilled nursing care throughout California. As of the May 26 amendment that were taken in the Senate, we removed our opposition to this bill, and we were neutral on this bill prior to this being heard in this Committee. Just wanted to echo that and again thank the Senator for meeting with us and his staff several times. I really appreciate working with you, and thank you again.

Thank you Mr. President. This Legislature has for many years, grappled with the very difficult challenge of mental health in incarceration, whether in state institutions or county institutions. It has been an extraordinary challenge. In Los Angeles County I believe it's the largest mental health facility in the country and that is the county jail. A big problem. And so, as we grapple with this, we continue to drill in to try to understand and to make it better. We know that the cost of providing service on the average is about $22,000 for somebody for mental health services and over $70,000 to incarcerate them. The District Attorney of Los Angeles came to me and uncovered another challenge with respect to the law and what we're doing here in SB 931 is correcting it. Basically under Lanterman Act, if you want to do a conservator-ship, if someone is safe, you get pushed to the bottom of the line. Being put in jail is considered safe. So what's happening is this odd framework. It's odd circumstances happening where well someone's in a safe condition and so as a result, they're not being treated. They're not being included into a conservator-ship. And so what we do is to just basically remove the idea of safety as a factor so that incarceration does not impede someone's attempts for treatment and conservator-ship. I ask for your support of this measure.

Of closing if you can't have the contracts, unless I'm not understanding the broader nature of this. Is there an issue that could disadvantage one facility in a multi facility system? And this is complicated and I'm sorry, I've been paying very close attention.

Yes, yes I am and let me begin by thanking this Committee for it's characteristically thoughtful analysis and by agreeing to the suggested amendment in comment three that the water replenishment district of Southern California be required to submit an independent audited financial statement annually to the Governor and appropriate committees of the Legislature including the Senate Governance and Finance Committee.

Yes, Brett Johnson, senior Director of policy and advocacy with the California Life Sciences Association. Not sure I can top that. So we're the life sciences sector composed at Biotech, medical devices, research institutions. And we're responsible for a great deal of the advancements by American Biotech. A lot of those advancements are coming right out of California laboratories. In just talking about our industry in terms of employment. We have direct employment of roughly 287,000 direct jobs and then we have another 597,000 that are indirect or induced jobs. So about a total of shy of 900,000 direct and indirect jobs. That we're responsible for. And thinking about California, a number of the top industries here in California are very STEM career heavy. You have electronics manufacturing, software, computers, aerospace, you name it. So California's a very high demand for STEM educated workers state. So of course for our industry, just talking a little bit about what we go through. It's very difficult to attract individuals with STEM backgrounds from outside of the state into California. For a lot of the reasons you heard on previous bills, it's a high cost of living here. So a lot of the tech sectors here in California rely heavily on our own homegrown STEM educated individuals. And just looking at that competition that we have within the state with other industries, it's really hard to get enough STEM educated individuals to meet the demand. Which looking forward is anticipated to be about twice that for STEM as non-STEM.

Thank you Madam Chair. One quick comment, I was not on the Subcommittee process so I did not have a chance to look at the item. I do appreciate the focus on upgrading the pure cannabis control CDTFA and Department of Public Health. But I did want to just raise this ongoing need for enforcement in those matters. And I think this was an item discussed in the budget sub Committee so pardon the request for refresher. But is that no longer in this budget now, the enforcement piece?

I want to thank you. And just before we go on to Mitch, I think that the and I think all of us who have experienced these fires, agree that the that the mutual aid programs, the coordination that the agencies have all demonstrate together has been extraordinary. I think without question, we have the finest emergency systems in place of anyone in the country, and thank you for that. And I also want to mention when you talk about prepositioning, I know that for the debris flows which occurred here, and which could occur, shall we start getting some significant rains throughout the state. Where those fires have completely destroyed all vegetation and created that instability in the hillsides, the night off their debris flow, I'm told over 900 people were saved because of pre positioning that you had done in anticipation of that happening. So the public, I think, should certainly feel reassured that the best that can be done is being done in terms of these kinds of lifesaving efforts by our first responders. And I think we all share in our appreciation for that. And of course going forward, there is much more that we need to do as these events become more and more dramatic.   You did mention something, it's not really on the agenda for today. That I think we're all concerned about is the impacts that these events are having on our first responders. Whether it's their mental health, we know we're seeing extraordinary and unusual number of First responders having serious mental health issues. Leading in some instances to suicide, also the fact that there's no recovery time or training time for this new abnormal that we're having to deal with. So I hope that we will take that into account. But the costs just the monetary cost, the cost to our children and the lack of opportunity to continue their education. Businesses that are losing their businesses, homes, people losing their homes, their valuables, their sense of hope. And security, I think, are all things that is we are going forward, we're gonna have to recognize and try to address, but certainly the work that's been done. And I'm sure with all these fires, the fact that the Woolsey fire, we did have three fatalities. But when you look at the extent of that, damage, as many of us have, I just driving up the 101 yesterday, seeing it in Calabasas and gore and jumped eight lanes of freeway and just, I know in Malibu similar things. The fact that there were three deaths is tragic. There could have been so many more, and while we mourn every one of those people, we have to recognize that the work that's being done has been extraordinary. So I wanted to at least share my observation on that, and thank you all for that. Next, we are now going to focus in on the emergency notification issue. And thank you a moment for your reports, and your great work. Mitch Medigovich, I'm going to get it yet Mitch. Who is the Deputy Director of logistics management with the Governor's Office of Emergency Services, will give us an overview of the recent changes to state law governing emergency notification. Thank you, Mitch.
Yes, Mr. Chair.
Thank you, thank you, Mr. Chair.
So because I'm fairly inexperienced with all of this stuff, being of the older generation, when you say the aggregate of this information, how is that information available in this aggregate form?
Niccolo De Luca here on behalf of the Oakland mayor in support and Moxie Extracts in support. Thank you.

I won't even take three minutes.- Sorry.- Good afternoon Chair Stone and Members of the Committee. As Senator Weiner said I'm Rafael Mandelman, I am a newly elected member of the San Francisco Board of Supervisors. And I'm here to express my strong support for SB 1045 and to thank Senator Weiner for taking on a difficult but very important bill. Over the last year of campaigning, talking to folks throughout my district, I've talked to a lot of people and no issue is more frustrating for my constituents and no public policy failure is more obvious, painful, and embarrassing to our city than our inability to provide care to so many obviously sick people on our streets and in our public spaces. For me it's personal, my mother suffered from mental illness much of her life and spent time in a homeless shelter when I was young. I know from personal experience that there are people who are unable to care for themselves. And it is unreasonable to expect those people to voluntarily access services and yet that is precisely what we do. As a law student I work to get institutionalized patients into the least restrictive living environment, but the least restrictive living environment cannot be a sidewalk. Too many of our neighbors live on a relentless and costly merry go round that carries them from the streets to the hospital, back to the streets, to jail, and around and around and around. And it is inhumane, it is expensive and it has to stop. San Francisco did not create this problem, but we are doing our best to solve it. Please give us this desperately needed tool to move the very sickest and most expensive folks off the streets and into care. Thank you.
It's been moved by Senator Portantino. The motion is, let me put this around here.
Madam Chair, Matt Ryan representing the Orange County Employees Association in support. Thank you.

Mr. Chair and Members of the Committee, for the record, Dan Reid with National Rifle Association, here in opposition to AB 3. Asides from the obvious policy concerns, this bill's going to increase costs in itigation. This is something that has been tried in courts across the US, as well as it's going to have a significant impact on California's wildlife. By prohibiting the ability to purchase firearms and ammunition for 18 to 20-year olds, you're alienating a group of hunters. These are thousands and thousands of hunters here in California. And if you can't buy a firearm or you can't purchase the ammunition necessary, it's going to impact them. And they're probably not going to be as likely to go out and get a hunting license, which is critical to California's wildlife. Also, you could be encouraging straw purchases. If someone's coming in to hunt from out of state, they're not 21 years old, how are they going to get the necessary ammunition to participate in California? Because you can't bring it in from out of state, so what are we doing there? As far as the wildlife, Pittman-Robertson dollars are, it's an 11% excise tax on firearms and ammunition, no matter what the use of it. And those dollars are then distributed based on hunting licence sales, so if we're going to have less hunters and less people buying firearms and ammunition, these projects go directly to benefit California's wildlife, and with that we're in opposition. Thank you.

Thank you, good afternoon, Chair Jackson, and fellow Committee Members. My name is Keith Casey, I'm Vice President of Market and Infrastructure Development at the California ISO. And among other things, I oversee the transmission planning work that goes on at the ISO. And I'm pleased to be here today to briefly share with you the activities we undertake. To enhance the state's electric grid against major disasters, such as major earthquakes. And importantly, to also highlight to you the partnership and the role we have with the transmission owners, the utilities that own and maintain the transmission facilities. So if you turn to slide two, it just gives you a quick overview of who the California ISO is and what we do. Essentially, we operate the power grid for most of California. Our operational footprint is highlighted in yellow on the map. Notable exceptions are noted, I would note in particular for LADWT and the area called Bank includes the SMUD, which you're residing in now. Those are not part of our operational control, but for the most part, our operational footprint comprises the transmission facilities of the three major utilities in California. Pacific Gas and Electric, Southern California Edison, and San Diego Gas and Electric. And importantly, our job is to manage the flow of power over those facilities. But the utility's still owned and maintained the transmission facilities. And prior to the formation of the ISO in 1997, the utilities operated their system separately. When the ISO came into formation, those systems were combined and operated by the ISO as one single large system. And you can think of our job as essentially akin to an air traffic controller. Where we're directing the output of the power plants to ultimately get to the load center. So we're managing the flow of electrons across those power lines. And we do this primarily through our centralized wholesale energy markets that enable us to optimally control the dispatch power plants to meet the needs of the system. So, let me move on to slide three, which, when it comes to grid resilience, it's important to understand our our role, versus the role of the investor-owned utilities. Specifically, a big, important part of our role at the California ISO is doing, as I mentioned, a long-term transmission planning. Where we're looking out at the needs of the system 10 years out, and improving transmission enhancements. To address, primarily, reliability needs on the system, but also to support state policy objectives, like the RPS goals, and economic needs. If there are bottlenecks in the system that are resulting in inefficiencies, we can improve transmission to address that. And importantly, our assessment also includes assessing the resilience of the grid in high-risk areas against extreme events like earthquakes. That could take out major transmission infrastructure, power plants for extended periods of time. And then lastly, we do coordinate with the utilities in developing system restoration plans. So if the system does go down, we have an agreed upon plan on the steps we'll take to re-energize it. And we do practice drills with the utilities on that. And then lastly, I would note we do work very closely with the utilities and emergency organizations, such as CalOES, FEMA, Homeland Security, on emergency preparedness training and scenario drills. And I would note, you've heard some of the drills discussed in the prior panels. PG&E is holding their annual drill on August 8th and 9th, that will simulate a major earthquake event in the San Francisco area. So we'll be actively participating in that drill as well. So let me talk about the role of investor owned utilities, because like I said, it's a complimentary role, an important one. They own and maintain the transmission facilities, so their key roles here are hardening the grid infrastructure. And what that means is really upgrading existing facilities or building new facilities to meet earthquake-resilient standards. So really, when I say hardening the system, it's really, just as you were talking about, building upgrades. It's making sure those substations and transmission lines can withstand major seismic events. And then, of course, operational readiness for when an event does strike, and I'll use the word not if, but when. That the utilities have robust restoration plans in place. So that they can quickly assess the damage to the system, and get field crews out to repair and replace damaged equipment. So a lot of these scenarios that they go through every year are really testing the robustness of those restoration plans. So a lot of slide four talks specifically about, in our planning studies, we've actually adopted a reliability standard that is unique to our planning requirements. And it goes over and above the national standards, to really get specifically that we have areas of the grid that have unique risk of extreme events. And the standard specifically calls out the San Francisco peninsula. And what makes that unique from a grid standpoint is really the combination of the things that you see listed in the sub bullets here. It's a high-density urban load area, obviously, but importantly, its geographic system configuration. Obviously, it's a peninsula, so it has radio feeds of transmission to serve that. Contrast that with the Los Angeles Basin, for instance, that area has multiple transmission coming in from the north and from the east. It's a much more mesh network, where the San Francisco Peninsula has limited transmission. Just kind of dead-ending at the cul De sac at the downtown area. And the obviously huge seismic risks for the Bay Area, and challenging restoration times. So when you have a catastrophic earthquake there, the restoration can be quite long. So in light of that, that criteria enabled us to do a study of the peninsula area, which I'll conclude with. But ultimately, it enabled us to approve a transmission upgrade for the San Francisco peninsula, to specifically mitigate the risk of a catastrophic earthquake. So the last slide just highlights the study we did in 2013 and 2014. Where we assessed the seismic risk of extreme earthquakes in and around the San Francisco Peninsula. We looked at four different scenarios in the 7.2 to 7.5 magnitude. There were two, San Andreas Fault events, one in Hayward, and one in San Gregorio. And what that study identified were really two big things, one, it highlighted that the The infrastructure in the peninsula itself, the transmission facilities there, which are primarily underground, many of those are frankly old and needing to be replaced. Along with that, a number of the substations in the peninsula also needed seismic upgrades to ensure their resiliency. So we really worked with PG&E collaboratively to identify where those critical risks were and agree with PG&E on an action plan for replacing the underground cables to the State of the art cables. Which are much more resilient in hardening all the substations in the peninsula, some of them had already been done, but we identified others that needed to be done. And then from our standpoint on the planning side, we identified San Francisco really has three power sources. They have a submarine cable that comes in right at Potrero. And then they have transmission lines coming up the peninsula from two different sources. One of them, well, all of them actually, to a certain extent, if you lose the substations they feed into, you lose all those transmission lines that connect to that substation. So to mitigate that risk for one substation, we agreed we would effectively pull one of the transmission lines out of the substation and route it around it. So that it had a direct path to a substation further north, so that if we lost that specific substation, we would no longer lose that transmission line. And that was determined to be a very cost-effective solution relative to building a brand new fourth source of transmission into the peninsula. So that kind of gives you a good example of how we collaborated with them doing enhancements as the owner of the facilities and us in our planning role as identifying improvements to help mitigate that risk. So I'll stop there, I know you're short on time. But I'm happy to answer questions as you see fit.

Thank you very much, Madam President, and happy Monday, members of the California State Senate. All of us, in our capacity as members of the Legislature, as members of budget committees, know the unique and important role and value of the Legislative Analyst's office, also known as the LAO. You might not know some facts I'm about to share with you. Mac Taylor has served as our LAO, our leg analyst, since October of 2008. And recently, in fact, he gave me a call, I think it was during break, to tell me that he had had a a-ha moment, and realized that he had served for a good number of years and that he was going to retire at the end of this calendar year. Prior to his appointment at the Leg Analysts, Mac began his career at the LAO in 1978 and was appointed a Deputy Legislative Analyst in 1990. So to do the math for you, colleagues, that's 40 years of uninterrupted public service specifically with our Leg Analyst's Office. This kind of dedication and longevity of public service, quite frankly, is rare in today's world. And I think we all should applaud Mac for his commitment to us as a legislative body, to the residents of the State of California based on his commitment through his distinguished career. The LAO has been providing fiscal and policy advice to the Legislature for 77 years. The office is highly regarded for its expertise and its nonpartisan analyses of fiscal issues impacting California. In Mac's role as the leader of LAO, he's overseen a team of approximately 43 analysts and 13 support staff. Their expertise covers all the critical components of the state's budget. They also play a central role in forecasting state revenues and expenditures and in estimating the fiscal effects of proposed initiatives. Mac is known within the LAO for his analytical rigor and his high standards, as well as his accessibility and his dedication to the office's mission. Personally, Mac is unassuming and kind. He lives in Carmichael with his wife, Sherry. He has three adult children and two grandchildren. I am assuming that he'll be able to see much more of them, particularly come January when the rest of the folks in the LAO's office will be buzzing. Mac and I have one thing in common, we're both Highlanders. He, too, is an alum of the University of California, Riverside. And it gives me great honor, as chair of the Joint Budget Committee that I represent you all on, to acknowledge a true public servant in the purest definition of that term. Someone who has committed himself to a bipartisan, bicameral approach to solid fiscal analysis and solid policymaking for every California resident. Please rise and acknowledge the retirement of Mac Taylor.

An argument is, well, we would have to go before the planning Commission and do all this, and a time consuming, and that's probably true. But the real challenge here for many of these companies as you eviscerated your public affairs entity of your company. And you no longer have relationships with these cities, because 20 years ago, we knew who our public affairs reps were. And they were able to walk in and talk to the mayor, talk to the planning department and get those permits, because I did that job. And most of your companies have now done away with it. So you're going to continue to have these challenges with local government, and that's why the league is opposed. Because most of them don't know who their reps are, who could have come down, sat down with your City Council, sat down with your planning department and our commissioners and clearly explained this. Because I did it and it was very easy having those relationships. But now, when you have a public affairs person responsible for 20 cities, you don't know 20 mayors. You don't know 80 City Council people, you just don't. And that's the problem, and I just think you now coming to the Legislature to ask us to grant you permission to do work in local government, in local cities, that really should be dealt with on that local level, but I understand the need. But I think the process needs to be improved as to your approach.

So the chair's recommending a no vote. So let me try to get through this and put it in way where we're not going at each other, because there's some young people that are being ravaged right now. So, at the end of the day, we've got to provide services. And what Senator Mitchell's bill, what Senator Mitchell's intent was to force us, adults in California, to provide the wraparound services that even though law enforcement and they're doing a commendable job at going after human trafficking. But even they will tell you, when they bring them in, they're not handing them off to all the proper wraparound services that they need to get out of this life, get away from the traffickers. That's what the bill is essentially saying. Don't criminalize those young people. Get them to the services that they need. Even you agree that we need to get them to the services that we need, not lock them up and criminalize them. That's the inherent difference between the two. I even heard from law enforcement that we do need to come together and talk to each other. Not scream at one another, not, not scream at one another, but to come together and provide the kind of services that are needed so that they don't go back into that life. We're not doing that adequately right now and we need to put more effort into that than unfortunately this bill provides. And again, I would offer, if you sincerely, genuinely want to help, that is the best way to help, is to get on board with us, providing the funding, and providing the mechanism, where we can take care of these young people, so they don't have to be back out on the street. And I think even, if you were to talk to your Republican colleagues who sincerely want to help young people, You will come up with, and probably work with us to come up with the methodology that we get rid of this And then we can allow law enforcement to go after the Johns, and the pimps that are preying on our young people and we go after them with every tool we can to stop it. But the other part of it is, we got to heal things young people. We can't criminalize them anymore, we got to heal them. And we got to bring them back in and we got to show compassion for them. And that's where we are right now. That's where we are in California. We have compassion for the victims. That we have compassion for people who are being victimized by this. And that's the direction we're going in. We've already tried criminalizing young people and we see where that's gotten us. Let's give this a try right now. As with that, I'm ready.

So this is a trailer bill proposal that we put forward so that we would have statutory authority to seek federal approval. To essentially continue to do what we have been doing. DHCS and our county eligibility workers have been using CalWORKS eligibility determinations to confer eligibility for Medi-Cal since 1999. When the ACA changed Medi-Cal eligibility rules to the modified adjusted gross income, it created some slight inconsistencies, but in our mind still really meant that if you're eligible for CalWORKS, you're eligible for the Medi-Cal program. CMS has raised some concerns about us, not having sought specific federal approval to continue to do that. So this trailer bill, essentially has the Legislator tell the department, go ahead and do that, so that we don't jeopardize, this really much more efficient way of determining eligibility for CalWORKS. So that the county eligible day workers, don't have to do two separate eligibility determinations.

Colleagues, several years ago, there was a debate with regard to the food trucks that we see in and around the capital and perhaps in your districts where we see food truck festivals. And there was a tremendous amount of push back, that they could be harmful and hurtful to the public health and wellbeing of those who would patronize these particular food trucks. And I think that's been a demystified argument. Several years ago there was the Cottage Food Industry Bill that has opened up opportunities for many individuals to establish these micro-enterprises, businesses, and has supported communities like yours and mine economically. And here we are today, the sky hasn't fallen. So we have this bill that builds on those efforts, that ultimately does, as I mentioned, three things, it protects the public health of people in California. Number two, it focuses on economic empowerment for people who are limited but can use their skillsets, apply them in a way in which they can provide a food good, a service. And then the third, that I've not mentioned, is that it decriminalizes something that already is happening. You may have read, or heard of a story of a young woman down the street in the county next door where she was arrested for selling ceviche. And prosecuted by the District Attorney. Now, I mean, I think that what we're doing here is very pragmatic and a commonsense approach to making sure that all of the public health regulations and inspections are in place. In order to, again, establish these micro-enterprises in our communities, protect the public health, and once again, decriminalize something that happens already in our communities. I respectfully ask for your aye vote.

Hi, I'm Cindy Blain with California ReLeaf, I'm the Executive Director. And I'm going to cover, sorry, I'm going to cover three aspects of urban forestry. First, I'm going to talk about ReLeaf's role in urban forestry and then I'm going to talk about our granting programs and a couple of the projects. And lastly, I'm going to cover some of the issues of the co-benefits, public health, capacity building, and jobs. For 30 years, California ReLeaf's been the umbrella organization for urban forests non-profits, grass roots non-profits, that plant and care for trees in their neighborhoods, using a model based on the belief that true change occurs at the local level with the local residents involved and making decisions. As you said earlier, small investments making big changes at the local level. The network right now is about 100 strong and includes groups like Tree San Diego, Urban Releaf, Puentes, Tree People, A Cleaner Greener East L.A., and several local Conservation Corps. We provide education advocacy for the network and any interested community benefit organization. It's not just somebody who normally plants trees but any group. Our primary statewide partners in these efforts include the Green Lining Institute, the Nature Conservancy, Transform, and Housing California. These partners work with us on integrated solutions for social equity and sustainable communities as well as environmental justice to bring trees to the communities where they're needed most. And the most important part about trees, the health benefits of those trees. So for 30 years, we've also administered grants. Currently, the majority of these grants are being supported by greenhouse gas reduction funding, but we also get some funding from other sources. Trees are cost-effective investments for capturing greenhouse gas emissions to mitigate climate change and for improving the health of residents. Because trees are filtering out particulate matter near high traffic roadways, which is all really burdened in disadvantaged communities, as well as helping protect urban neighborhoods from extreme weather events that climate change brings. With extreme heat and air quality being a greatest concern to public health professionals at this point in time. And again, this is something that overly affects disadvantaged communities because you're starting to talk about utility costs. And if we don't have trees, we don't have evapotranspiration and all those cooling benefits. So trees are a critical component of climate adaptation toolkit for designing healthy cities and building community resiliency. So when the greenhouse gas reduction funding became available in 2014, ReLeaf focused on developing partnerships between community groups in the under-served neighborhoods and more established urban forest non-profits, the buddy system, essentially. To say that we needed to develop capacity for urban forest projects in under-resourced neighborhoods and this was the best way to do it. With the help of local organizations, we began a program of deep outreach into disadvantaged communities so that the funding would go to those organizations in the neighborhoods. Let me give you an example of that. One of them is a great example of that is a partnership between an urban forest non-profit, Urban Releaf in Oakland and the community benefit organization Episcopal Impact Fund working in one of the neighborhoods. The program is called Greening Santiago and Our Neighbors, which is planting 92 trees in a community garden. That Episcopal Impact Fund is also partnering with Iglesia de Santiago, a low income Spanish speaking congregation, the Alameda County Food Bank, which serves mostly Asian Americans and Indigenous Permaculture, a group working on food security. Getting together adverse partnerships in communities is incredibly powerful and bringing residents together from different backgrounds and strengthening the community overall. Planting trees is an easy introduction to community activism and environmentalism. And it has been shown that community activism leads to civic engagement as well as long-term stewardship of these trees. To continue, as far as grant support. It's important to underscore the unique ways in which the urban forestry program has been able to successfully reach and benefit disadvantaged communities through out the state. ReLeaf has been to able model and successfully implement capacity building at the local level by providing technical assistance to small groups that otherwise would have missed the opportunity to benefit from cap and trade. For small community organizations, it can be a serious challenge to submit a government grant application for a variety of reasons, including simply awareness about the program, capacity, and technical expertise. Because we do pass-through grants or we take, we get funding from car fire and we do grants to the small organizations, in 2016, only 40% of the applications we received from disadvantaged communities were eligible to be reviewed. That meant they had everything they needed, simply to be reviewed. So because our mission is to empower grass roots efforts, that led to our policy of supporting grantees to make sure that their applications were eligible without yet taking into account the substance and merit of the application. We are literally teaching grant preparation skills and building capacity because we know that this will go far. We worked with all applicants to provide technical assistance in completing various unfamiliar components, like fiscal sponsorship, quantification of carbon sequestration values for projects, and obtaining permission to plant forms from cities. Learning the system is what really needs to be taught in some of these communities, or learned in some of these communities. As a result, we were able to increase the percentage of eligible applications from 40% to 85%. And of course, after grant award we continue to support grantees throughout the entire project to completion. A couple examples of new community groups we're working with our Fathers and Family of San Joaquin and Stockton as well as Parent Pioneers in San Fernando. Let me give you one other example of another project happening. Amigos de los Rios, now this is a more established urban forest group in Southern California who has an incredibly ambitious vision of the emerald necklace linking more than 1500 acres or parks and open spaces along 3 different rivers in LA. Amigos is doing it methodically, segment by segment. The funding that they received from California ReLeaf of $70,000 to plant 230 trees is for the Whittier Narrows project running along two of the rivers and two massive freeways where there's some of America's worst air pollution and some of the most populated urban areas in the United States. Having lost trees to drought and invasive pests, they're restoring these trees by partnering with high school service clubs, Girl Scouts, San Gabriel Valley Conservation Corps. One of the Conservation Corps young men assigned to it, Ricardo Dobart II took the bus at 6:30 AM from his home in Azusa to come plant trees here. He dropped out of high school but later graduated while working on another Job Corps program. But Ricardo explained that he didn't really like school and would rather do something more physical. Working with plants, trees, and nature helped Ricardo to find a sense of belonging he hadn't felt before, he said. And planting in trees and being in nature, even urban nature, helped him deal with frustration and anger. His new attitude was reflected in his approach to digging in dirt that's rock hard due to drought and our climate, our arid climate. He said it was hard at first, but then you learn how to do it. Then you start to enjoy it, which is a great life skill anywhere. This leads us to our last topic, which is jobs. And we heard over and over again, the trees are wonderful as we were talking about these projects and communities. And the under-served communities want the trees, but they want jobs. And several of the existing urban forest projects are providing wages. For example, the Urban Corps of San Diego is doing that in partnership with Tree San Diego, Sustainable Claremont has the Green Crew program and high school students at Earth Team and Madera Coalition for Community Justice. Those are non-profit jobs based on grants. It's more important to have steady jobs. And so it was a marriage made in heaven when we heard that the tree care industry is having a dire shortage of tree care workers. And they were willing to expend a lot of time and effort to provide training for this. So we are supporting a project with local tree care companies, Merritt College in Oakland, as well as the Tree Care Industry Association to develop an urban arborist certificate program as well as an associate of science curriculum that provides a pathway to stable, good paying jobs. And these are jobs that were stable even when we had the recession. And the minimum wage in the Bay Area, anywhere from 15 to $17, so it's not the minimum wage. I'm saying the starting salary much better than the minimum wage at this point. The workforce development curriculum and sector partnership model will soon be available to all interested California community colleges so we can take this model and use it in other parts of the city. What was really important about this is that you had the tree care companies involved and they're the ones that are going to hire. And they wanted to be there and make sure that the program was set up right so that people were ready to be hired right off the bat. So we all know that planting trees is great and now we're beginning to plant jobs as well. Thank you very much for you time today and for your interest in this as well as your leadership in cap and trade.

Thank you, there's a motion in the second. Good afternoon Mr. Chair and Members of the Committee. My name's June Lopez and I'm with Moulton Niguel Water District, and I thank you for this opportunity to spoke to speak in support of the long term efficiency framework. I'm not speaking in support or in opposition to any of the bills because I do realize that with all the discussion it might be helpful to have a little brief recap of how we got here and what the framework is trying to do. The last three years have been very challenging, but we did a lot and we Learned a lot from From that. And some of the lessons that we Learned, is that percentage reduction across the board is very challenging for water agencies. Because it doesn't recognize the past and present investment in water efficiency programs. We also heard from our stakeholders, our customers, the public, who really didn't understand how the percentages apply to them in terms of individual action in achieving efficiency. So we talked about this and variety of other elements during the urban advisory group, which was a cross section of stakeholders, which I and several people in the room have had the privilege of being members of. And we knew that the framework wasn't perfect. I'm not speaking in strong support because it's perfect, but it's a movement in the right direction. And we need to move now before we lose the momentum, the valuable momentum that we created with the public in the last three years because they get it. They know that the emergency is lifted, but we're not out of the woods yet and there are more conditions ahead, historic dry conditions, so we need to prepare now. So, we don't want to lose that drumbeat that we've built over the last three years, and that's what this efficiency standard does. And it's not that new, our agencies, as well as several other agencies in the room, have applied this methodology in achieving efficiency and we've had great success. Because it's not about using less, it's about wasting less. And the public understands that. And if you could identify in efficient use and you target those, and you recognize those who are efficient, then everyone benefits. And we also have gotten some very good data from this type of conjunctive level approach. The data that we could use not only to understand how water is being used, and how programs and projects to work, but also to balance. How much can you do with demand management strategy and how much infrastructure do you need to build to have supplies available when you need it? And having this data has allowed us in the last two years save $20 million because we're able to identify those opportunities where we didn't have to build expensive infrastructure. And we're able to leverage partnerships with agencies like Facebook and Netflix to really work together to have data drive decision for better and smart investment. And they're a lot of things that we need to work out and I'll look forward to being part of the dialog going forward and all the iteration talking to agencies across the state and learning and partnering together.

text: In our case we don't have the investment managers come to the Subcommittee on investments. They come and visit us in the office of the CIO, because the way investment decision making is delegated, is to the investments team. And so the board of regions get involved in asset allocation decisions, broadly speaking. In terms of whether we're invested in stocks or bonds or other types of assets. But not at the level of a specific choice we would make on a particular investment manager. So we do have the dialogue, but that doesn't happen at the border level. It happens in our offices. And we do engage with BlackRock, in fact, more frequently than even on a quarterly basis. As an example, we have usually their managers tells us about how our investments and how things are going around the world. And to everything you've said, I have to tell you this is probably one of the most challenging, it's a challenging time to make heads and tails out of what is going on globally. In fact, we don't even have to look outside our own country to figure out how complicated the correlation is between the stock market and the political environment. And that complexity translates everywhere in the world, frankly. No more deeply than even in the emerging markets. But we are very actively engaged in these conversations almost monthly at this stage.

Thank you. I want to thank Chairs Pan and Chairs Berman for convening this. I think that the fact that you see two houses here, both Senate and Assembly together, with three different committees coming together, is really important. Because it emphasizes how much this issue means to the California Legislature, and to the State of California. So I want to thank all those who convened this, all those that worked on the Committee, the staff, all of the presenters. Thank you for being here, coming from all different places. I think your messages were loud and clear. We heard loud and clear that no one size fits all, that there needs to be flexibility, and that our approach has to be multi-faceted, not just looking at municipalities or counties, but also CBOs and nonprofits altogether. We heard that it's important that we have coordination between these entities, and that that coordination has started. We also heard multiple times how important grants were versus contracts. You all did manage to coordinate that message well. Message delivered. As well as making sure that California's investment is an investment that goes into existing efforts that have a proven track record, and I think that that was important. We also know that there are certain areas of California that are hard to reach. And those areas include the Inland Empire, the Imperial County, Central Valley, rural communities and fire-stricken areas. And I think that all of these message, along with the multitude of details are really going to help inform and move the conversation forward. I do want to, again, thank the Chairs of the Census Committees in both houses for including the nonprofit sector in this conversation, allowing us to have a more robust discussion about how we move forward as a state. So thank you.

Good afternoon, Adrienne Shilton with the Steinberg Institute. Our mission is to promote sound public policy and inspire leadership on the issues of mental health. We're the proud sponsor of this bill. In the era of integrated healthcare it really is imperative that all medical professionals are trained at recognizing the early signs and symptoms of mental health issues. And particularly related to children and our young adults. This is especially important for pediatricians and general practitioners, to ensure that they're fully supportive in identifying mental health challenges early and appropriately treating them. We strongly believe that AB 1340 is a practical approach, to not only meet the increasing demand for mental health services that we are seeing. But also ensuring that physicians have the expertise to treat clients appropriately. As Mr. Maienschein said, we have data that show that 50% the first onset of mental illness occurs before the age of 14. At the age of 24, that rate increases to 75%. And these individuals are typically presenting in the primary care setting. So in closing, supporting early treatment in primary care settings will reduce the incidence of undiagnosed mental illness, as well as reduce the prevalence of untreated mental illness and produce better outcomes for our clients. So we're really striving at the Steinberg Institute to ensure that the mind is treated equal to the body when it comes to care. And AB 1340 would equip physicians with the tools and knowledge they need to meet the demands. So with me today to also testify in support of the bill is Dr. Jay Han. He's the Program Director of Family Medicine and Psychiatry at UC Davis. He teaches, practices, and research in the areas of integrated healthcare. So I'll turn this over to him.

Today, we're here to address the critical issue regarding the deadline set by Ed Code 33000 in relation to the selection process of the student board member on the State Board of Education. One of the many successes in the history of amplifying student voice in the education arena was the addition of the student board member on the California State Board of Education in 1984. Since then, students have been given the opportunity to voice their concerns, attitudes, and educated opinions on the pressing issues facing our school system. The SBM stands as a liaison between students and educators. They share the power of decision making, which ultimately leads to a newfound channel of communication between school officials and their students. It has been decades since the California Association of Student Councils has been collaborating with the State Board of Education in terms of expediting the selection process for the student board member on the State Board of Education. Around the first week of November, CASC directs the Student Advisory Board on Education, the sister conference to SABLE. At SABE, students have the opportunity to construct thoughtful proposals, similar to the ones presented to you today, to the State Board of Education. One of the most unique aspects of that conference is that delegates have the opportunity to communicate their needs, concerns, and thoughts directly to one of their future representatives on the State Board of Education. This student board member faces the burden of fully and honestly representing the 6.3 million students of California. Therefore, it would be irrational for us to allow them to carry the burden of understanding a complex selection process in the matter of few days. In the beauty of the conference and the delegate satisfaction, we have forgotten an equally important dilemma. The high levels of stress the Student Board member candidates suffer simply due to the deadline of the application being set so late into their Junior year of high school. As the current ED CODE 33000 fails to address this concerns. The California Association of Student Councils proposes that this state Legislature amend the education code to set more specific and timely deadlines in the SPM selection process. An amended ed code should include an initial notification date of May 31st, a followup notification date no later than August 31st. The application, due by September 31st, and the release of the governor's final appointment, no later than March 31st. Let's break it down a little. Currently, the ed code does not state an initial notification date by which all school district governing boards District Superintendents, High School Principals, High School Senior Activities Directors and Student Body Presidents should be informed of the position and it's job description. We are recommending that the date be set, May 31st of the student's sophomore year, so that they have adequate time to assess their situation and compile an application that reflects an understanding of the commitment they are offering. In the same manner, the ed code currently states that all parties should be notified by latest September 25th. However, we are recommending that this date be moved back to August 31st, so that students are reminded again at the beginning of their junior year. As so, we further recommend to change the complete application deadline from October 31 to September 31, as well as, demarcating that the student should be informed of their status as the state SBM no later than March 31 of their junior year. There is one main reason as to why these set dates for the SBM selection process for earlier than awaited is now. These students deserve the right to make other commitment if their seat is not secured on the state board of education. The truth is that the SPM finalist, the three of them, who have to wait all the way until late May or late June to hear the results avoid applying for other demanding roles that they are passionate about upon the hope of that they are still in the running for the position. This change in that line will allow these students to run for other student council positions, commit to other extra curricular activities, and pick a course load in relation to their availability. Additionally, by adding a preliminary date for informing school officials about the position, we are catalyzing conversation earlier and are offering the candidates enough time to evaluate their role for the coming year. Essentially, this proposal will get cast, the State Board of Education, the State Senate, and Governor Brown, from November to March of the students' junior year to approve the SBM elect for the coming year. In summation, the amendment ed code 33000, that include specific and earlier deadlines, will ease the time against race for all parties involved in the election of the student member to the state board of education. With that, I yield to questions.
My name is Elizabeth Stites and I oppose this bill as amended.

Like, I mean we really need to start evaluating how we look at things. And technology is moving in a whole new direction. One of our employers or you guys call them clients, we call them employers, power chair's broke. She came up here anyway, wrote a whole thing for you guys. Nothing will stop their abilities, but it's like don't take away, don't take away from the things that they need. They need this. It's really ain't just so much about the staff, it's about them. Dental appointments, Doctor's appointments. You would be so surprised that how many people that I support that I have to argue and fight with a Doctor just to get a mammogram, just to get a Pap smear, just to get a real physical. Do you know how hard that is, to fight for somebody to get a physical? And it's bad, it's extremely bad. And all the Doctor can sit here and say is, well, it's not in our budget. Well, we don't cover it. I'm like, and what type of Doctor are you, don't you go by ethics and codes? Then they'll start to help when I start to challenge them. But I have to go through so much just to get someone to help somebody that is disabled. How fair is that to them? How fair is that? I've been doing this since eight years old so I can just imagine the frustration, the aggravation. So this is years of aggravation that you guys get, sorry. But it's like, you gotta be able to help. And you gotta be able to evaluate each situation. Not just say, well, we gave them money this year, so they don't get none.

I don't want to speak to Glyphosate directly, because it is in litigation. I'm going to turn to Carol to fill you in on that. We do have four listing mechanisms. We have two independent panels appointed by the Governor. Very expert in carcinogenicity and reproductive toxicity. They add chemicals to the list. We have an authoritative bodies mechanism, where bodies that they identify as authoritative. Mixed findings. We review them. There's a involved public process. So there's that process. There's incredible overlap between that and the Labor Code process, where IARC, the International Agency for Research on Cancer, is both an authoritative body, but also, the Labor Code triggers listings of things by IARC that are identified as having sufficient evidence of cancer in animals or humans and not to speak directly about Roundup but just to speak in general that the intensity of the scientific reviews by IARC is extremely high. They look at the three main streams of evidence, mechanistic evidence, human evidence, and animal evidence. They're extremely competent, so they have made scientific findings is all I'm trying to point out.
