So I've prepared a statement here, which I'd like to read and go through some slides talking about the industry. My name is Sean Casey. Serve several roles, one of which is I head up the Silicon Valley Space Center, which is an incubator and accelerator for early stage companies in the space sector. Prior to Silicon Valley Space Center, I spent 15 plus years working at NASA Ames on the airborne astronomy program. My professional background is as an airborne astronomer having been at NASA Goddard and also at NASA Ames. SOFIA, the Stratosphere Observatory for Infrared Astronomy, is now based in Palmdale. And so I've had the pleasure of getting down there and knowing some of our aerospace colleagues in that area, aside from being up here in Northern California. I've been asked to speak about the NewSpace Industry, which is colloquially what people refer to as entrepreneurial space. We see a lot of press associated with some of the companies that have presented previously, SpaceX and Virgin Galactic. Also, there's Blue Origin and Stratolaunch. I feel that there is an emphasis on what, quote, the billionaires are doing, in this industry, with less of an emphasis on what the smaller, mere mortal entrepreneurs are doing. I think the space entrepreneurship depends upon those less-monied individuals and their role to function in our Silicon Valley society. Which we've known is very popular for Google and Facebook and some of those companies. I've also asked Arwin to share with the Committee some reports that I've run across that are from Goldman-Sachs, The Bryce Group, and Space Angels, about the growth of the new space industry. I sent these to Arwin this morning, so they may not be in your packets today. But I think those are important to talk about how the industry is growing. I have also had the opportunity to work with the Commercial Space Flight Federation in Washington DC, and have, in that capacity, traveled around the country talking to a variety of groups in Florida and Texas and New York and other states. And I see a growing momentum of space entrepreneurship, not just here in California but elsewhere. One of the slides I'd like to present is a funding diagram of venture capital as it's allocated into this sector. There are several big high points for the last couple of years. Generally between 2005 and 2015, you've seen a bubbling of interest within the venture capital community, probably maxing out at about $500 million per year, oscillating it between $200 and $500. But in 15, 16, and 17, we've seen banner years for venture capital investment. Some of those numbers are dominated by SpaceX and OneWeb. But what we've also seen in a growing interest in the smaller startups, which are shown here, and certainly in 2016 and 2017, we've seen a growth of funding for smaller startups. OneWeb is a constellation of over 800 satellites that are poised to provide global Internet coverage, and indeed, the growth of the new space industry may hang upon the success of OneWeb and some other companies. I would note that the OneWeb constellation is actually conceived of here in California, whereas the satellite construction is actually moving down to Florida for that constellation. The technologies that have been important in this growing new space sector are the same technologies that have been disrupting a wide variety of industries. This is a report from 2013, from McKinsey, talking about disruptive technologies. And I list those here because we see those same things applied to the small satellite sector. In particular, mobile computing technologies, mobile communications, the ability of AI and robotics, along with additive manufacturing, and solar panel improvements and efficiency. So a good example of how that is impacting our industry is with a company, one of the earliest space startups called Planet Labs. It's now called Planet, and I show here some of the founders. Will Marshall and Chris Boshuizen, with Steve Jorbertson an angel investor and venture capital investor that's well known here in Silicon Valley. Steve is actually holding one of the early Dove satellites, which is a CubeSat technology based upon a concept developed by research faculty Bob Twigs at Stanford University. This technology, pretty much been around for a number of years prior to Planet's adoption. But both Will and Chris working at NASA Ames were very familiar with Pete Wharton's vision of small satellites as they applied to the defense industry. So Chris and Will and others spun out of NASA Ames to form Planet. And there was a great deal of uncertainty and consternation about the utility of small satellites in low Earth orbit. The fact that you're in low earth orbit means that you have a benign radiation environment. And that allows you to use what are terrestrial technologies at those altitudes. So Planet initially flew a few satellites and today has grown to over 200 satellites in low Earth orbit that provide a twice daily snapshot of the planet. As an astronomer, I know that every time you build a new observational facility, you're going to derive new results and a better understanding of whatever we happen to be observing, in this case it's the Earth. Planet has also leveraged a growing supply chain. And with secondary launch capabilities on vehicles like SpaceX and others, they're able to deploy these satellites in low Earth orbit. I show here the satellites being deployed from the International Space Station, which was a relatively novel concept and pioneered by the small satellite community. Here an entire payload of small satellites are shipped up to the International Space Station, and then deployed by astronauts on a boom shown here. A company called NanoRacks actually serves the International Space Station and this small satellite community for the deployment of satellites in lower orbit. The satellites, as you can see with Steve, are about the size of a bread box. The technology is able to develop that a price point which is amenable to venture capital investment. So what's happened with new space is not that venture capitalists have embraced space. It's that space entrepreneurship has moved in to the funding envelope, which is typical of a Silicon Valley startup, with a seed round, a series a, a series b, a series c. Say, half a million, $1.5 million, tens of millions of dollars. And able to demonstrate technology milestones and retire market risk, while they're taking in tranches of funding. An example of Planet's enabled capability these days. And Planet has also gone through a mode of acquiring and the growth of the company from its start in 2011 to today has pulled in about $200 million. They've developed their small satellite program and they've also acquired some additional satellite facilities. This is an animation that I hope this comes up here, but let me rewind it back. A series of images taken with the Planet facilities over a period of months showing the build out of the Tesla Gigafactory. The data that you get from these programs, let's just get rid of this. The data from these programs assists both certainly, for eyes on the construction industry, agriculture, mining, finance. And I have a number of other examples which, for the brevity of this presentation, I have not included. But satellite imaging allows you to have transparency into our economy unlike what we've had before at a national and global level. It also allows us transparency into economies where we might not have that kind of transparency. What is the oil use in China or coal use in India? Planet, with the development of the small satellite program, has actually, pioneered the use of these technologies for a wide variety of other firms, many of which are here in the Bay Area. Companies that I have listed here for imaging companies are Planet and Astro Digital and Satellogic, they're all looking at unique verticals to serve in markets of agriculture and video. Synthetic-aperture radar is being done by Capella Space. There are technologies which show promise in space which haven't been flown that have been developed by entrepreneurs. Communication companies like Audacity and Astranis. GPS occultation companies like Spire we're able to measure properties of the Earth's atmosphere. And data analysis companies, companies that do not actually have hardware, but are using the data that comes from other satellite companies and making that last mile play to customers. Space debris from Leo Labs and Made in Space focusing on, on orbit manufacturing. I think Made in Space is a unique company because it's actually pioneered its technology using suborbital flights before deploying to the International Space Station. The fiber that they're planning on making has the benefit of being high throughput and low absorption coefficients. To enable broad spectrum and then high data rates, unlike what we've seen previously. When I look at vehicles like Virgin Galactic and their use as a research platform, not only is it jst for tourists and educators, but for B2B play. Made in Space did about 400 parabolas on the zero G vehicle to prove out their 3D printing technology. This was an important use of that vehicle before going to the International Space Station With this, we expect to see a wide variety of small satellite companies, as I've shown in the last slide. I would say over the next five years it's projected that we'll see about a three x increase in small satellites in low Earth orbit. There are a number of incubators and accelerators. For instance, the Founder's Institute has their Star Fellowship program that expects to graduate over 500 new space startups over the next seven years. This is Adeo Ressi, the founder of Founder's Institute speaking with Steve Jurvetson, one of the early investors in Planet Labs. All of the companies that I had listed previously are early, seed-stage, Series A, Series B, companies.
all_names: Sean Casey | Will Marshall | Chris Boshuizen | Steve Jorbertson | Bob Twigs | Pete Wharton | Adeo Ressi | Steve Jurvetson

text: Good morning Mr. Chair, members. Mark Fenstermaker on behalf of the National Parks Conservation Association standing in strong support of AB 1000 today. This bill is essential to protect the California desert from the proposed Cadiz water project which seeks to drain an ancient aquifer, which is vital to life in the Mojave Desert. Project proponents are working with the Trump administration to allow the project to evade federal environmental review, NEPA which was previously required under the Obama administration. This is a dangerous press stand, only made possible due to numerous conflicts of interests between Trump officials and the project, we urge your support to move this bill off the suspense file, thank you.
Thank you very much, thank you very much. Thank you, I ask everyone, please, to respect the rules that we need to lay down here, so that we can get through everyone in line. Thank you, next speaker.

Yes, thank you, just also to clarify that employers can also now download the posters from DFEH as well, so that gives us another opportunity. And again, members, this is such a critical bill for our transgender, gender-nonconforming individuals and particularly when you look at our transgender community of color. And the high rates of discrimination, the high rates of assault, and particularly sexual assault in the workforce, which I believe is almost close to 80% of our transgender community, have suffered some sort of discrimination or abuse at the workforce. Sometimes even for just using the restroom. And so, this might not seem an important thing for us individually, but it will make a world of a difference for our transgender community, and again, put California as a national leader when it comes to protecting our gender-nonconforming and our transgender community. And I often say, if the T in LGBT is going to stand for anything, we have to stand with our transgender community. With that, I respectfully ask for your aye vote.

In opening our gathering today, please let us close our eyes in a moment of reflection. I've always found it interesting that the idea of being human is often used to describe what is great and not so great about us as a species. In excusing our weaknesses or our failings, we might hear the phrase, he's only human, she's only human. Yet, when one manifests what is most noble about our shared humanity, we might hear them praised with the phrase, she is a true human being. He is a true human being. When his holiness the Dalai Lama honored us with an unprecedented visit last June, with a deep warmest smile, he greeted everyone with his palms together. This is a very traditional gesture of greeting throughout Asia. It is a gesture of both greeting and deep respect, but in that simple gesture, he was greeting not nearly our physical presence but also bowing with deep respect to our potential to awaken, awaken to our true and shared humanity. With palms together he was greeting that part of us that is only human, but more deeply with his respectful bow, reminding us of the potential we have within us, to manifest our true humanity. Since that unforgettable meeting, I know I will always feel his warm gaze, and see his palms together greeting me, and reminding me to awaken my potential to be a true human being. I have no doubt that in his heart the Dalai Lama is forever humbly bowing with palms together to all of us and to all the world wishing for us all to manifest what is most noble about our shared humanity and how [FOREIGN] means with gratitude beyond words. Thank you very much.
Okay, so far as your policy position, you think this is a good policy decision?
James Akers, I'm sorry.

Yes, just real quickly, I do sympathize clearly with the problem that he has identified. I think it's a real one, but the real problem I have was mentioned by the two opponents here about the affordability of people who get caught in this net, being pretty broad. And the affordability, their ability to actually pay. I'm going to not be able to support it today, but if you're able to get those amendments that may change.

Great, and I've got Steve Lindley, who is the Director of the Bureau of Firearms, who is here with me, as well, who can give you more details. I know that the $24 million that you mentioned, I'm not familiar with the $70 million that you mentioned that we got.  We got $24 million a few years back, to start to really deal with the backlog because, as you mentioned, every day there are more weapons for us to collect, because there are more people who are on this list, who no longer have the right to possess these firearms. If we had done nothing, we would have some, Steve, correct me if I'm wrong, but it's 40 some thousand or so. Here we go, we got a nice handy chart here. The blue line shows you where we were, before we were able to start collecting weapons, and as you can see, we would be over 47,000 weapons that would be out there, in the hands of people who don't have the right--

Thank you, Mr. Chair. What a busy day here in public safety. Thank you for having me. Mr. Chair and members, thank you for considering Senate Bill 776. According to the California Department of Corrections and Rehabilitation, California's total in custody population is approximately 128,000 inmates which includes approximately 6,800 incarcerated veterans or about 5.3% of the total prison population. It's always disheartening when anyone runs a foul of the law and ends up in prison, but it's particularly so and this happens to a veteran of our armed forces. However, we have found that in course a veteran who successfully reclaimed their veteran identities tend to be better citizen inmates. And also upon their release from prison tend to have significantly lower recidivism rates. Furthermore, access to veteran's benefits, especially VA healthcare, low income pensions, and disability compensation provide a vital financial framework to buttress incarcerated veterans when they are released and work to reintegrate back into general society. As you are probably aware, veterans who have already received VA benefits lose most of them during their period of incarceration, but can apply to restart those benefits upon release. The process of reinstatement can be challenging and often requires expert technical support. Other incarcerated veterans have never applied for earned benefits, and therefore need to apply initially during incarceration so that the benefits are ready to be turned on and available when they are released. That takes even greater technical support. County Veteran Service Officers, or CVSOs, as they are known in the trade, are the most important resource in helping veterans access federal and state benefits. But CVSOs are underfunded and overworked in many of the rural counties where prisons are likely to be located. We have Learned that inside the prison, veterans support resource is the best way to supplement and coordinate with the local county CVSO office. The bill before you would require CDCR to crosstrain several non-custodial employees to assist incarcerated veterans with managing the veteran's claim's for benefit. The bill before you today authorizes one position for every five prisons, which would be a total of seven positions. SB 776, bless you, would require the employees to be trained and accredited through CalVet, our State Department of Veterans Affairs. CalVet already trains and certifies CVSOs and other vet reps. SB 776 would require that CDCR vet reps have access to all computer technology necessary to perform their claims and duties. SB 776 also provides that the CDCR workers input their claims data into the VetPro system. VetPro is the veteran case management software used by CalVet and by our county veteran service officers to help ensure standardization, accountability, and quality control. SB 776 is sponsored by the California Commander State Veteran's Council. And it's also supported by major veterans groups, the ACLU, the California Public Defenders Association, and California Attorneys for Criminal Justice. I have with me today, Mr. Pete Conaty, representing a number of veterans organizations, and I respectfully ask for your aye vote today.

I'd like to ask for unanimous consent to have 30,000 petitions Prop on the floor here if I may have them delivered. I have over 30,000 petitions against SB 54. And I would like to have consent to bring them to the floor.

Mr. Mcguire, so it will not be heard today. And we're in a smaller room today. So I know people want to support the testimony of others, but we need to be able to hear everyone. So If you want to show support to a speaker, you can do the American Sign Language applause. And please don't clap or exclaim out loud, I would appreciate that. All right, and then we've got, let's see, I don't think there's anything different on the consent calendar. Let me see what members we have in the room. Mr. Newman, all right, we will start with you, Mr. Newman, and that is item 17, SB 1242. Yeah, establish a quorum first, apologies... Okay, no problem I have to call the roll anyway. Roll call. Skinner? I'm here. Skinner here. Anderson? Here. Anderson here, Bradford? Here. Bradford here, Jackson? Mitchell? Stone? Here. Stone here, Wiener? All right, so Mr. Anderson or Mr. Bradford, do you want to start?  All right, what about you, Mr. Anderson?  I know, I know. Well, heck I could start.

text: I'm Jessica Gauger with the California Association of Sanitation Agencies. We wanted to mention that we're here today neutral as amended, with the proposed amendments in the analysis. We'd like to thank the Committee for working with us and the author on those proposed amendments related to the disposal issues, we think they allow for the appropriate level of local discretion, so we really appreciate that. We'd be happy to answer any technical questions relative to the sewage disposal as well.
That's my close, I'm done. All right.

This is the fee structure, I think also some judges gave me a list similar to this of how complicated it is and where everybody gets a penny here, a nickel there, and a quarter here, and what have you. And it really is a very convoluted system. But also, as I pointed out, it is a system that kind of feeds on itself because it's in the self-interest of those who collect the fees to have fees, if they're the ones distributing the resources and get some of the resources through other operations. So it really is an unfortunate thing. And I know we've seen a lot of unfortunate things happen to people because they couldn't pay and we're looking at this whole issue of do we have a debtor's prison whether it's our bail system or whatever, lots of folks were incarcerated for minor offenses because they couldn't afford to pay. And that's probably a tragedy right there. Because they'll never be able to pay and so this begins a cycle a downward spiral of losing license, losing jobs, losing resources, losing their ability to relate to their children because they can't pay child support and they've lost their home. I mean it just goes down and down. I think we see this a little bit particularly in the veteran community, that's when they have that stand down every year. I know the district attorneys and the judges are there to dismiss all these things that people have because there's no way you're going to collect all this money from folks that are homeless. And they've got multiple misdemeanors and felonies and things because they were homeless and at some point they just dismissed all of that stuff to try to get people a clear slate from which they can begin to rebuild because, every time they get ready to crawl up, it's all these other things that are added on. So we're looking at the system and I know that it's very complicated but some of the recommendations were really quite good. Thank you very much. I don't know if there's anyone in the public who would like to make a comment over any of the items, if so, please line up, you'll have a minute to tell us where you're from and very quickly what your position is. And we're over time but we'll take the three or four folks who've come, please.

Thank you very much, Chairman Pan and Members of the Committee. My name is Dr. John D Stobo, and I'm executive Vice President of UC Health. I appreciate the opportunity to share a few thoughts with you today. UC Health is made up of 5 world-class academic health centers, 11 hospitals and 18 health professional schools. Today I have been asked to provide an update to last year's testimony regarding our experience with Medi-Cal. Medi-Cal is critically important to the state's growing population, keeping the state's growing population healthy. UC Health continues to make extraordinary commitments to the Medi-Cal population, and we are pleased to do so. UC is the third largest provider of hospital inpatient services to Medi-Cal beneficiaries, yet our hospitals represent less than 6% of acute care hospitals in California. We have also become the fourth largest provider of outpatient services to Medi-Cal beneficiaries in the state. Last year I testified that UC Health absorbed more than $700 million in unreimbursed Medi-Cal costs. In 2017, that figure grew to nearly $900 million.  This financial commitment, I feel, is unprecedented. To address this shortfall, the Legislature and the state Department of Health Care Services partnered with the University of California and other public hospitals to pursue federal matching funds, which are critical supplemental payments. Remember, our medical centers receive no state General Funds, so our reimbursed cost must be offset by other revenues. We are please to report progress in four areas, compared to our report last year. First, we have been able to achieve a Master Services Agreement with the three largest Medi-Cal managed plans in Southern California, L.A. Care, Inland Empire, and CalOptima. This should result in more robust interactions between three medical centers in the south, UCLA, UC-Irvine, and UC San Diego, with these managed Medi-Cal plans. And make the movement of the Medi-Cal patients from our facilities to the plans much smoother.  Second, we reported that UC Davis entered into an agreement with United Health Care last year to provide Medi-Cal patients with primary and specialty care. This contract was recently expanded to include more Medi-Cal beneficiaries. Third, through our leveraging skill of value initiative, which is a system-wide cost reduction initiative, we've achieved up to 800 million in savings, up from just over 500 million last year. And our target is to have $500 million of savings each year. Lastly, although patients come to UC typically for more highly specialized and advanced services, we understand the importance and the need to increase primary care. We have tried to do so, many through partnerships with community-based providers, community-based primary care providers and facilities, such as the partnership we have with El Centro in Southern California. As we mentioned UC Davis is a particular area of interest to us. UC Davis has been able to enter into agreements with H Federally qualified Health Centers in the Sacramento area under the Elisha name. And UC Davis Children's Hospital is just marked their one-year anniversary with a relationship with Sacramento Primary Care Center. So while we have made some progress at UC Davis, we feel that we still have a way to go. In closing, through our mission of training future physicians, we have expanded our touchpoints well beyond the walls of UC hospitals. Our doctors rotate to many sites of care away from our campuses. At any moment about 20% of our residence are in county hospitals, 20% are in community hospitals, and another 20% are in VA facilities. These training programs are of the highest caliber. According to the 2018 US News and World report, 3 of our schools ranked in the top 10 nationally, in primary care education. Over 30% of our residents are training in primary care specialtities. We appreciate the opportunity to be with you today and I'm happy to answer any questions. Thank you.

Good morning, Shari McHugh, McHugh, Koepke & Associates, here today on behalf of the California Self Storage Association. I would first like to start off by thanking the Chairman, the author, and their staff for working with us on the amendments, and we're happy to be here today in support of the bill. I have with me this morning Erin King, who is the Executive Director of the California Self Storage Association to briefly outline the specifics of the bill. Thank you.
Hi, I am Alissa Medina, I'm with Ray's Policy and Law Academy of Oakland Technical High School, and I support.

Mr. Chair and Members, Curt Child, Disability Rights California. I just want to first thank the Chair and the Chair's staff and the author of the efforts that they've made. First of all, reducing this bill to what it is, a study now, and an effort to make the study a little more balanced in its approach. Unfortunately though, we have to continue to oppose this bill. And it's really quite simple from our perspective, is that this along with the litany of bills that we constantly see each year, really becomes an effort to try and limit the integrated community services and residential facilities and options for persons with disabilities. And so while this study might be posed as looking for some protections for those who are in the treatment programs, what we really believe is behind this is again, searching for some documentation to begin to limit. There'll be the next bill that comes down to limit the ability of individuals living in a least restrictive environment in their communities. And so for that reason, we would continue to oppose any of these efforts. Because it does ultimately undercut what we think is a fundamental civil rights issue for persons with disabilities. So we would urge your no vote on the bill.

Thank you very much, the bill that was cited as the justification for this bill from the last session from the President pro tem in regards to the AQMD. The editorial board of the Los Angeles Times refer to that as stacking the AQMD board. The reason why that bill came around is because counties like mine, counties in the Inland Empire region were deciding who their own representatives on the AQMD board, people that maybe didn't reflect the values of Sacramento. Now the AQMD board is a hybrid, some are appointed from up here in Sacramento, and some are elected from our local elected officials down in Southern California. So Sacramento saw a reason to stack that board in the phrasing of the LA Times, the CTC is all appointed by Sacramento, there's no shortage of social justice warriors that are already on this Committee. So why do we need this stacking effort on this side?

Just want to say thank you, Chair Holden, for the conversations that we've had, and the time that you've put in. Have been very educational and informational for me, and appreciate the chance at a Hail Mary. Again existing law requires that the CPUC ensure that there is no cost shifting. And we are committed to that. And appreciate and respectfully ask for your aye vote, thank you.

Thank you Mr. Speaker, I rise very briefly in support of ACR 11. Colleagues, this is an important and consequential measure. It's been stated by the author and the previous speaker, cancer is not very discriminatory. It has no respect to a person, to borrow a phrase. And this measure, what it encourages is an important part of what we can do as a state to promote healthy lifestyles and living, and to begin the drumbeat to end cancer, to find cures across various types. California is uniquely positioned to be able to make advancements, so on the treatment, diagnosis of cancer, because of our institutions of higher education, and because of the diverse populations that we have that have particular susceptibility or higher rates of cancer. So it's really heartening to see this measure brought forward by the representative of the Dublin area. And I would hope that this measure moves forward, and that we all figure out ways that we can promote innovation, and work toward a cure across all portions of this state. It will make a measurable difference in the lives of Californians. Thank you, Mr. Speaker. I respectfully request that an aye vote of ACR 11.
Mr Mccarty? Here. Mr Chavez? Here. Mr. Muratsuchi? Here. Mr Voepel? Here. Mr Medina. Here.

Good afternoon. I'm John Gundry. I'm Superintendent of the Santa Clara County Office of Education, and I appreciate the opportunity to address The Housing and Education Committee on this important matter. California is currently in the middle of a growing teacher shortage that we expect to become severe in the next couple of years. A number of factors are affecting our ability to attract and retain qualified teachers to the Bay Area and not to least of which is the high cost of housing. The high cost of living in Santa Clara County is a challenge for local school districts because teachers are reluctant to accept a position in a place where they cannot afford housing. In Santa Clara County the income required to own a single family home is more than a $170,000, while the average teacher salary is between 80 and 90,000. The substantial gap is the number one reason why it's extremely difficult for counties such as mine to recruit and retain teachers. In San Francisco, first year teachers spend about three-quarters of their salary on a median priced one bedroom apartment. The situation is not much better in the Silicon Valley, San Jose area where beginning teachers will have to spend about 50% of their salary for a similar apartment. AB 1182 will help high cost counties like Santa Clara attract teachers who would otherwise be deterred from teaching in an area where living in the community is simply cost prohibitive without financial assistance. In order to solve the state's teacher shortage, we have to address this issue with a multi-prong strategy that includes legislative initiatives, such as AB 1182 and AB 45. Students suffer when a teacher is forced to live far away from the schools where they work. Many teachers in Silicon Valley commute from as far away as Stockton and other communities in the Central Valley where housing is still affordable. AB 1182 will allow more teachers to live in and be part of the community in which they teach. We greatly appreciate your fine work to support our students and staff in school districts across California.

Good afternoon, my name is Fabiola Martin, I am a mother of five boys, my oldest son has autism, and I live in Los Angeles. I am here today to speak to support AB 3086, I currently work as a substitute teacher and I also provide behavioral therapy for children with autism.  I'm here to speak today to support the Bill 3086 because I believe it's important for children with families that are navigating through challenging situations and could be able to stay in or seek out a public school that fits best, no matter which district those schools are in. I know what it's like to deal with an unstable housing and struggle with homelessness. In 2012, following a series of difficult circumstances, I became homeless myself. During this period we moved around a lot and my children were moved between schools within a district as well as schools across district. At one point my eldest was moved, was assigned three different schools in one week, these schools were across town, and I do not have a car. Eventually I was able to enroll my children in a school that is meeting their needs, and this means a lot to me. They are stable and in a high quality school environment, I don't have to navigate through another system. I am thankful for this school because they accepted my children with their disabilities and they were able to create and I was able to create Continuity and maintain a community by having a stable education environment for them. Trying to navigate homelessness and, or the foster youth system means doing the best you can with the limited options available. And trying to get whatever resources are offered from big bureaucracies. For my children, being able to stay in schools that are fit for them has created a lot of stability for my family. Even with the other parts of our lives that are very chaotic. AB 36...

Yeah, just a couple of comments. There's a lot of important points that have been made here. And I don't want to diminish the importance of getting this right. And I don't want to diminish the urgency of it. But I think we are certainly dealing with a situation, as I described, where we're breaking new ground, we are figuring things out as we go. And if we were looking to build water storage projects that met some specific power generation need, there's a recipe in some engineering book somewhere that someone would look through and flip through and say, here's what we're going to do. And so I know a number of my NGO colleagues and I have been involved along the way, providing input. We felt that the staff has been quite responsive to our input and the Commission has been responsive. But there are things that I would like to have seen in there, in the regulations, for instance, that I didn't see. But I'm sure everyone who's been involved in the process can probably highlight some of those. I do think, if I had had the chance to do it, I would probably do it a little differently, but I probably also can't be certain that the way I did it would've worked any better. And I'm certain that we would have had kinks to workout in the process. So all that to say, I think there is good reasons that we're struggling with this overall. I encourage the Commission to do their best to be responsive in the timeframe given. And as I indicated earlier, it doesn't seem unreasonable to me that to have a little more time, although I know that probably goes against the preferences of some of the other interests involved. But I do think it's a reasonable expectation that there have been blips and very much encourages us all to try to provide input to move the process forward in a thoughtful way.
Mr. Chair and member's, Kevin Baker for the Americans Civil Liberties Union of California and it's over 300,000 members many of them in your districts in support of the original bill.
Apologize that I'm late, the Assembly elevator was pretty busy today.

Alright, so this will be very brief. I am happy to report that in my experience on the board, we are encouraged to ask questions, to interact with staff. We do not need to go through the Chancellor. When we go on campus visits, we meet without the presidents with various groups on the campus. We have great access to staff members. And I feel that the questions we ask and the things we bring up are treated as important and that we get a response. So, this is an ongoing thing. Medium, got it. Mr. Chair and members, Sylvia Saleesha on behalf of the California Faculty Association, we are the sponsors of this measure. We represent 28,000 faculty, librarians, coaches and counselors in the CSU system. A system that serves more than 470,000 students in the State of California. Here are a few reasons why we are sponsoring this measure. The CSC was hit hard with budget cuts from the recession, and CFA has worked very hard to get state funding levels increased. In his press conference on the release of the 2018-29 state budget this January, Governor Brown, when discussing the issue of state funding for the CSU and UC. He indicated that he believed that the CSU and UC systems both needed to lower their cost structures and to live within their means. CFA is grateful for the recent increase that was included in the state budget, and we believe that the CSU should become a more transparent system. It is unclear how the Legislature will provide the necessary oversight to the system when we don't have detailed information from the system or on a campus by campus basis. Allowing legislatures and the public to know more about how the CSU spends its funds will allow legislators and the public to hold the system more accountable. As was noted, AB 2505 is a result of a state audit that was issued late last year, which found that the current CSU budget process to be lacking in transparency and oversight. In addition to the budget process, CFA believes it is important for the CSU to provide additional data on hiring decisions, admissions, and enrollment information. Specifically, legislators have consistently asked CFA for the numbers of qualified students that were admitted into the CSU but we're ultimately not offered a spot in the system. They have asked us for information such as where these students applied from, like their County of residence, campuses that they applied to, and demographic information about them. We believe that legislators and the public should have this information. So for these reasons, we ask for your support of this measure. Thank you.

I'm Steve Kay from University of California San Francisco. I'm Professor at the Institute for Health and Aging. Is the microphone working? And long term care. We call it long-term services and supports is not just nursing homes, not just institutional care. Mostly it's people who live in their homes in the communities and they get help from people. Mostly it's not people who get help from public programs like in home supportive services, it's people who get help from family members. And a lot of people get a mix of both. It's not just elderly people, it's non-elderly people who have disabilities, and the goal of much of it is really to keep people in their homes and communities, which is what everybody wants. The vast majority of people would like to stay in their homes and avoid institutionalization. And it saves money too. And what LTSS does is it helps people maintain their health, their ability to function, their independence to the extent possible. Their self-determination, and their ability to participate as active members in their communities, and avoid the isolation that a lot of people, especially seniors, often have. So as we just heard there is an awful lot we dont know about long term services and supports in California. I can tell you that roughly somewhere between 1 and 2 million people in California need LTS right now, but I can't tell you precisely because neither the federal government nor the state does data collection  which we actually accurately measure the number of people who need LTSS. And there's an awful lot of stuff we don't know about those populations. One of which is, do they have family members who can help them, right? Or are they say living alone without family nearby and they're much more likely to enter a public program?  Much more likely to apply for in home supportive services. And if they qualify, get on. And we don't know what their income is so we don't know well, are they likely to qualify, do they likely to spin down, can they? And one major, very major thing is we don't know in general whether people's LTSS needs are being met or not. So, in among people not in public programs in California we actually know nothing. We don't know what percentage of those people would say that their needs are met versus unmet. In public programs, we've just done a study of people who are duals who are eligible for both MediCal and Medicare. And even among these program participants, most of whom are getting IHSS, if they need it.  42% said they did not get all the help they needed with their daily activities, which is 42% for people who are participating in major public programs. And bad things happen to people when they don't get all the help they need. For example they take the wrong medications or don't take their medications.  They have to stay in bed or at home because nobody is there to help them get out and participate in something. They don't get to the toilet on time, they end up in clothes that are soiled. They don't eat the food they need, and it happens a lot. So the other main thing that we don't know is why people are not getting the help they need.  We don't know what the barriers are and if we don't know what the barriers are we can't solve it. So we don't know what the solution is. For example, what are the supports that family caregivers need so that they can keep doing what they do and do it better and more efficiently. We don't know that. We don't know, are their alternative programs, is there something we could do, a program we could implement that would be something other than IHSS so that people who need a little bit of help, people who need support, would be able to get that at not some huge amount of cost? We don't know if there's some private solution like making long-term care insurance  something that people would actually want and could afford. And one of the biggest things is, we don't know are there solutions like technology home modifications? Are there innovations that could really help people and decrease the burden of care-giving and of the state to have to pay for these services.  So we're asking for a supplement to add questions about LTSS to the California Health Degree survey. Which is a very robust, well respected, existing survey in California of the overall population which provides county level estimates very well. And it already is very well set up to just add a few questions. And then, the people at UCLA will go and develop a questionnaire with the help of an Advisory Committee, and then field the questionnaire, following up with those people who identified in the main survey as having LTSS needs. Plus there'll be some additional, detailed interviews, detailed personal interviews with people who have high levels of LTSS needs to find out more about their individual situations. I would say this is money well spent because we really know so little and the need is urgent right now because, Baby Boomers is like me all aging into, needing more, into having more and more likelihood of needing LTSS, in the near future. And, if California doesn't start to plan for this now the budget will be broken. I mean at some time there will be so many people applying for LTSS, we will not know how to deal with it. We need an alternative.

Yes, Mr. Chair and members, John Doherty on behalf of the Civil Justice Association of California. want to thank you for your time today. The author, and Assembly woman, is bringing forward an important problem.  We're all aware of it. It needs attention, focus and actual solutions. But unfortunately, every proposed solution is not the right one for all the parties involved.  As written, AB 3080 creates an untenable dynamic. It undercuts the ability to settle cases and prohibits nondisclosure agreements, even when both parties desire them. The vast majority of filed civil litigation is settled by a voluntary and willing agreement between the parties.  California has long had a smart and important policy that actually encourages settlement that reduces conflict because amicable resolution is the best way to handle these things. And on top of that, it preserves very important, precious public resources. Section four of the bill specifically undercuts the ability to settle a case. The effective prohibition, which may be unintentional, but the effective prohibition in the bill on nondisclosure agreements is also problematic.  The California Fair Employment and Housing claims are some of the most sensitive issues that are litigated today, and eliminating NDAs will not protect claimants.  For those reasons, and the reasons articulated by the Chamber of Commerce, we respectfully ask for your no vote.

Good afternoon Madam Chair and Committee Members. I am pleased to present AB 655 which classifies waste energy facilities as eligible to participate in the California renewables portfolio standard. So long as the facility annually operates at least 20% below pollutant concentration limits outlined in the facility permit. There are currently only three transformational facilities in California, including one in my district, that complement the state's recycling efforts by diverting waste from landfills using waste to energy technology. This technology is internationally recognized by numerous governmental entities including the USPCA, European Union, I believe we were in the presence of one when we were in Ireland, Madam Chair, not too long ago, Cal Recycle, as a tool that can help mitigate climate change relative to landfilling. These facilities provide multiple environmental benefits to the state including carbon dioxide savings, resulting from shorter transportation distances. A net reduction on methane emissions from the reduce need for landfilling and a clean source of low to zero carbon based load energy as opposed to fossil fuel based energy. Despite these advantages, landfill waste is recognized as a resource for renewable energy generation, while that same waste taken to a transformational facility, is not. This is despite the fact that these facilities use the same waste stream as landfills, reduce GHG emissions, have extensive pollution control equipment and continuous emission monitoring not in place at landfills. The lack of a renewable energy designation precludes waste energy facilities from entering into new and long term contracts which provide the stability needed to sustain their operations. AB 655 seeks to recognize the environmental benefits attributable to municipal transformational facilities, by classifying the energy produced at these facilities as renewable energy. This classification is consistent with the state's commitment to reducing landfilling, reduce greenhouse gas emissions, and reduce methane gas productions and emissions. And here with me today is the Mayor of Long Beach, Honorable Robert Garcia, Kevin Norton from IBEW Local 11 and Ellie Booth with Covanta Energy.

This bill simply creates a road map for the budget process. For 80 years we had a road map that was called horse racing. Profits from horse racing were then a portion of them were shuttled to fairs. For the last several years, we've had no road map and so this, by creating essentially a formula where the Board of Legislation annually reports gross sales, that will give a budget format. Thank you, Mr. Speaker and members. Colleagues, I ask today that we adjourn in memory of a remarkable young woman who was taken far before her time, Alycia Aly Yeoman. Aly, a young 20 year old student from Gridley was known for her big heart and big smile. Aly was loved by all, and was an extremely hard working young woman. Working two jobs at the local Starbucks and Mcdonald's while attending Yuba College. On March 30th, Aly went missing, days later her truck was found abandoned in Orchard. The community rallied to help locate this young woman. Search parties and Facebook were mobilized to bring about her safe return. Unfortunately on May 7th, our worst nightmares were realized when her body was found along the Feather River. This has hit our community very hard. Aly had an infectious personality and there have been many stories shared in the last few months about people who were moved by her friendship, by a smile and a kind word at the Starbucks in Gridley. But I think more so, it was the snuffing out of such a bright light. Someone who had such great promise and a full life ahead of her, that hurts so much. Alycia was well loved and celebrated by her community. A group has recently been formed, Justice for Aly, who are over 8300 members strong and this group seeks to honor her memory by finding the causes of her untimely death. Someone out there knows something, and we encourage people to come forward and give law enforcement more information. So that we can find the truth about Aly's untimely death. A celebration of life was held for Aly on June 3rd, where hundreds of family, friends and community members wore white and yellow in honor of Aly and her favorite flower, daisies. Though the circumstances around her death are still unknown, her parents, family, and friends should know that the condolences of this legislative body go out to them. Today, we share in their great loss, and I ask that we adjourn in her memory. Our hearts broken and our thoughts with her community, her family, friends and loved ones. Thank you, members. I ask today that we adjourn in memory of Aly Yeoman.

I recently saw that California is paying three times the national average for every road mile of maintenance in the state and that's unacceptable. The Legislature can't treat taxpayers like ATMs who just dispense money to government whenever Sacramento demands it, with no accountability. I want a commitment to cost savings reforms, so our families and businesses and drivers get a better rate of return on our investments. Reforms in our labor rules, environmental regulations, design bill regulations, and anything else that will let us do it better for the people paying for these bills. Also I know that our own Legislative Analyst has identified roughly $500 million of waste at Caltrans and that the state diverts about $1 billion in wait fees away from building roads and into the general fund. Any request for new money needs to be viewed through the lens of the current money that we are wasting or misappropriating. Lastly, I know that this hearing is not about high speed rail but it stands alone as a symbol of waste with cost estimates already spiraling out of control. Thank you, Madam President. To implement provisions of the state administration and Cannabis Trader bills and to make various technical changes to budget items. Specifically, the bill clarifies the resources available to each of the four members of the Board of Equalization. To ensure that each member is authorized the same number of staff in 1718 as they employed on June 15th of 2017. The bill also requires a report from the Department of Finance to the Legislature that reflects an evaluation of ongoing staffing needs. It provides a total of $5.6 million to the Bureau of Cannabis Control, the Department of Public Health and Department of Food and Agriculture, to implement responsibilities associated with the Cannabis Trader bill. These include establishing quality assurance and tax verification inspectors, maintaining the state medical identification card, and establishing an office to meet the needs of residents on the north coast. It appropriates $19 million from the Prop 1 water bond, $49 million from the Santa Monica Mountains Conservancy, and $49 million for the San Gabriel and lower Los Angeles rivers and mountains conservancy. It also makes various technical changes consistent with the intent of the 2017 budget act.

So we all have different responsibilities, state, federal, local. We all come to that with different missions. But I will tell you that in California in particular. We have an extremely close working relationship. There are federal partners in particular, the forest service are a part of forest management task force.  They are part of the watershed improvement program that Jim talked about, very critical partner in watershed projects because in California issues watersheds, fires, insects and disease know no boundaries. And so using tools such as the good neighbor authority, which the forest service has been very open in working with us in developing.  These are all tools by which we're sharing resources and working across forest boundaries. Yes, sometimes we may be at odds between the state's role and mission on private land and federal mission and responsibility and public policy. But it's our responsibility collectively to be at the table. And work through those and not let that be the impediment at the boundary to get this work done. And I think all the efforts that were talked about today are really leading the way in breaking all those barriers down and getting the work done.

Because it sounded to me that while there were mistakes it was also very evident that our public safety can't live without the product. And so that is part of the necessity to try to figure out how to move forward. I know it's also very complicated. On August 9th I was in Lassen County and I got evacuated for a fire and there was a moment where my phone was the only one that was working and multiple people were borrowing the phone and it happened to be that I am a customer of yours. So it's complicated, we don't know when or where things might happen but I would really hope that what comes out of today is not just a focus on one company but a way for all of us to move forward for every company to know that this is something that is important to us. It is something that is our new normal as the Governor has described it. So we want to ensure that we never put public safety or the people of California in a place where those minutes, and seconds count and that communication is vital to it. I want to thank you for your quick response to this problem, I think that that is something that does not go unnoticed, that the response was very quick to try to rectify a problem. I know that we can't always plan, create a law or policy for human error. It's hard, it's really hard. It even happens amongst us but it is so. Right? We're like I pressed the wrong button let me go back and fix it. It's important to recognize that again it's not limited to you but this is, it was important enough for all of us to be here in this room to have this conversation and to understand how we move forward. I do welcome that if there are additional conversations that needs to happen outside of this particular hearing, conversations that not only inform how we move forward that that maybe change the way we move forward to help public safety, I know that I would be interested in having those conversations if we have learned best practices that should be examined, that should be looked at in a way that this will not be the conversation we will be having in five or 10 years. We want to know those because we want to know how we replicate them across the state. I know what it's like to, you know, be evacuated not just in Santa Barbara and Ventura County but now in a rural, very, very rural part of California and that's harder. There are some other hardships there and so I think that as we have those conversations, I hope you will be at the table, it looks like you're going to be at the table, it sounds like you're going to be at the table and I thank you for that response. Although I'm not employed by any dialysis organization, I'm a medical director and attending physician. And I can tell you that, when breaks are taken, they're almost always not during time of transition because that's the most busy time during a clinic's operation. You have patients coming and going. You have patients who suddenly, would develop bleeding from a fistula needle site that has been clotted before. So, staffing during transition is almost always a full staff. I do take exception to what is proposed right now, that a charge nurse or a clinic manager, who is either a nurse or a former patient care tech, are not allowed to come and help out during periods of staffing shortage, to meet the staffing ratio requirement. I think that that rigidity places enormous burden on how to recruit staff. Right now in California, we not only have a nursing staffing shortage, we also have a major problem of recruiting able and willing dialysis nurses. And so rigid staffing ratio will place a huge burden on California clinics on how we recruit able, and willing, certified nurses and technicians to help out, at a time when the dialysis population is too slowly growing.

What's kind of assumed as part kind of the other strategies that are already being implemented by the state. So I'll just move to the final page, page four of the handout. Really just to highlight a few of the key issues, we think the Committee should be thinking about and considering as it conducts its oversight work, and one is just ensuring oversight and evaluation of the major climate policies. So to date, we have found no real sort of robust evaluations of the overall statewide effects of each of these policies that have been implemented so far, including both the sort of benefits and the costs associated with each of these policies. And the Legislature might want to consider, for example, creating some sort of independent Committee of experts to kind of help get at this kind of retrospective evaluation of the policies that have been implemented so far for each one. What have been the benefits and what have been the costs? That's, admittedly, a difficult process to try and tease out sort of all the different economic factors and other things that have kind of affected emissions over time, and that's why we think enlisting the help of experts and academics to do that could provide a lot of value. Second, an issue I talked on, the uncertainty around greenhouse gas reductions and cost out to 2030, just thinking about how different policies balance those uncertainties and potentially mitigate some of the uncertainties around either emissions or cost out to 2030. And then finally as you're evaluating the different options that were laid out on the previous page, some of the criteria include cost effectiveness of achieving the stated goal. The likelihood of each policy encouraging other jurisdictions to take action given that California represents only 1% of global greenhouse gas emissions, effects on other pollutants such as co-pollutants and toxics, and distributional effects. So the benefits and the costs of all these different policies and how they affect either different industries or different types of households. So I will end my comments there and happy to answer any questions.

Just to quickly touch on Massachusetts because it was brought up and it's a state that gets pointed out. They do a great job on, they lead the nation in a lot of different categories, including graduation rates, test scores in Math and in English. Not just in their general ed kids but in their at risk kids too.  And the way they did that back in 93 is there was a Committee meeting talking about the state accountability system. And there was another Committee in a different room in the Legislature talking about the school funding formula. There happened to be two people who were on both committees and they said, we need to talk about these together. So they created a funding formula that was based on their accountability system, and we would say that is what you should do. You should put those two things hand in hand. So they talked about things like professional development, and they said that's important. So there's a line item in there. For professional development money that every school gets per kid. They've talked about teachers in the classroom, technology, all these things that they felt were needed to get schools, to allow their kids to get to state standards. But then what they did is they say, we're going to make sure this money is available to all of you. But it's going to be you who decides how to spend it. So we're not going to say prove to us you spent $50 for getting professional development. If you spend less and your achievement is high, we're great with that. We are just going to care about your achievement. We're going to provide you enough money to get to where you need to go. We're not going to hold this over you. We're just going to worry about achievement and what they will tell you too though is they raised the bar for kids and they raised their achievements standards pretty high, and they suffered for years for bad press. Because they were getting 30, 40% of their kids scoring proficient or above. And they held on, and they said we're going to keep having these high standards, we're going to make sure the money's available to school districts, and we're just going to push everyone to get there. We know you have the money to get there. We're just going to push on that. What we would say is, think of outcomes not inputs, and if you did provide 22 to 40 billion in additional dollars, or whatever the exact amount is, don't think about micromanaging. Think about student achievement, what you want, and have those expectations. Now, if you give all this money and a district doesn't achieve. We'll hold that off of them and that's what Massachusetts does. They come back and say, you're not achieving at the rate that we think you should be. They increase funding. They increase funding. Right. So those things go hand and hand. If you're going to have higher expectations, you somehow need to cost it out to make sure you're providing the money to get to those higher expectations. I'm saying with this $22-$40 billion sort of thing. Some things we would say you do need to mandate but it's more the wrap-around services, just insure that things like. For certain districts there is at least one nurse in the school, per school size. That there are some of these other food services and things that are provided. But other than those things don't mandate, just provide the resources and provide guidance to get there. And again, try is best you can don't inter link your educational expectations with your funding formula.

To answer your first question, one of the biggest differences between preliminary hearings and grand juries is the timing of when that proceeding happens. A preliminary hearing is post charging, probable cause hearing. So the DA has to have developed at least probable cause and ethically, by our standards, beyond reasonable doubt, to actually go forward and file charges with the court. Then you have a hearing, if there's probable cause, the case goes to an information and to trial. Grand jury is a pre-charging situation which we often use in these tough investigative cases where it's not obvious what the facts are. Or the forensics don't support what the witnesses say, or there are conflicting witnesses. And there's not cell phone video that makes it definitive. In that situation, the grand jury is truly investigative, and it's ethically appropriate to consider possible charges. Whereas in the situation under 227 enforces the preliminary hearings, if we did not have probable cause, we could not bring the case forward. If we do our job properly and we believed in our cause and the grand jury agrees with it, we get an indictment and the witnesses name will become public, unless one party or another moves to redact part of the proceedings, which the current law allows. In this new law, it is still a secret proceeding. The witness is still forced to come by subpoena, testifies under oath there is actually rules and evidence to deploy. The grand jury shall not hear any evidence except that which is admissible by law, that's the law of the state. Only after 10 days for the transcript to be prepared, plus 10 more days for the defense attorney to get the transcript Would it at it's earliest become public? And there is still a provision on the rules of court, amendment that we've added to this bill. If it's a serious witness secrecy type safety issue, maybe a gang bang type situation where a report of a gang shooting another gang. And officers respond. Find a man with a gun. Shoot him. And you've got witnesses from rival gangs forced to testify. We have the time to put someone in a witness protection program. Move them before any name would become public. And if may not if we can redact that name from the transcript if a court agrees. But for the most part, we are trying to open these up. The very issues of witness secrecy that you raised were what convinced the Courts of New York, even after the ACLU sued in two different levels of court, to not release the Staten Island grand jury. We don't have any idea of what happened there. We do at least in Ferguson. And we are ready to do that in California too.
